**Step 1** ŌĆö Open a new VI.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Front Panel:** place a Numeric Control named `Celsius` and a Numeric Indicator named `Fahrenheit`.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Block Diagram:** drop three arithmetic nodes ŌĆö *Multiply*, *Divide*, *Add* ŌĆö from `Functions ŌåÆ Numeric`. Create three numeric constants `9`, `5`, and `32` by right-clicking the input pins of these nodes and choosing **Create ŌåÆ Constant**.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Wiring (the crucial part):**

- `Celsius` ŌåÆ Multiply (input A)
- Constant `9` ŌåÆ Multiply (input B)
- Multiply (output) ŌåÆ Divide (input A)
- Constant `5` ŌåÆ Divide (input B)
- Divide (output) ŌåÆ Add (input A)
- Constant `32` ŌåÆ Add (input B)
- Add (output) ŌåÆ `Fahrenheit`

The above wiring is a direct graphical realization of the formula.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Icon & Connector Pane:** edit the icon to a small thermometer symbol; assign a 2├Ś2 connector pattern: `Celsius` ŌåÆ top-left pin (input), `Fahrenheit` ŌåÆ top-right pin (output), plus `error in` ŌåÆ bottom-left, `error out` ŌåÆ bottom-right.

**Step 6** ŌĆö Save as `C_to_F_Converter.vi`.

**Step 7** ŌĆö Run. Test with `Celsius = 100`; expected `Fahrenheit = 212`.

**Verification Table:**

| `Celsius` (┬░C) | `Fahrenheit` (┬░F) |
|---|---|
| 0 | **32** |
| 25 | **77** |
| 100 | **212** |
| -40 | **-40** *(the famous fixed point)* |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most commonly lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing the three windows** ŌĆö Writing *"Front Panel is the program code"* is a direct loss of 2 to 3 marks. The Front Panel is the *user interface*; the Block Diagram is the *code*.
> 2. **Forgetting to draw both the Front Panel AND the Block Diagram** in 7/14-mark questions. KTU examiners explicitly ask for *both* ŌĆö a 7-mark sub-part can give at most **3 marks** for showing only one of the two.
> 3. **Missing the Icon and Connector Pane** ŌĆö A common omission when describing how to make a Sub-VI. Always describe *the icon*, *the connector pattern*, and *the pin assignment step*.
> 4. **Not labelling controls and indicators** on the sketch ŌĆö A sketch with unlabelled circles/squares earns only partial credit. Always *name* every control and indicator.
> 5. **Ignoring the dataflow rule** ŌĆö Stating *"the program executes line by line"* is a fatal error. The correct answer is *"nodes execute when their inputs are ready"*.
> 6. **Forgetting the mechanical-action setting** for Boolean controls in STOP-button questions ŌĆö The examiner expects *"Latch-When-Released"*, not *"Switch"*.
> 7. **Using the wrong wire type for the wrong data type** ŌĆö Orange wires are floating-point; blue wires are integer; mixing them creates a coercion dot which the examiner will penalise.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (high-density, exam-oriented):**
> - A **VI** in LabVIEW = **Front Panel + Block Diagram + Icon & Connector Pane**.
> - **Front Panel** = GUI = *Controls* (inputs) + *Indicators* (outputs). Grey window.
> - **Block Diagram** = Graphical source code. White window. Contains **nodes**, **terminals**, **wires**.
> - **Control terminal** has an *outward* arrow; **Indicator terminal** has an *inward* arrow.
> - **Dataflow rule:** A node runs *only* when *all* its inputs are available. *No implicit line-by-line execution.*
> - **Wire colours:** Orange (float), Blue (integer), Green (Boolean), Pink (String), Brown (Dynamic), White (Cluster).
> - **Coercion dot** = grey dot on a wire = automatic numeric type conversion = *avoid by explicit conversion*.
> - **Icon** = small graphic that represents a VI inside another VI.
> - **Connector Pane** = pin pattern that defines the input/output calling interface of a Sub-VI.
> - **Sub-VI** = a VI used as a callable node (analogue of a function in C).
> - **Error cluster** is always on **bottom-left** (input) and **bottom-right** (output) of the connector pane.
> - **STOP button** should use the **Latch-When-Released** mechanical action, not *Switch*.
> - **Standard 7-step construction algorithm:** New VI ŌåÆ Design FP ŌåÆ Open BD ŌåÆ Wire Logic ŌåÆ Edit Icon/Connector ŌåÆ Save ŌåÆ Run.
> - **C-to-F formula** (most-tested): $T_{^{\circ}F} = (T_{^{\circ}C} \times 9/5) + 32$. Wired using **Multiply ŌåÆ Divide ŌåÆ Add** chain.
> - **Run arrow** is *white* if the VI is ready to run, *broken* if the Block Diagram has errors. Click the *broken arrow* to see the Error List.
> - LabVIEW file extension: **`.vi`**. LabVIEW project extension: **`.lvproj`**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating an Icon
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT745 - VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Creating an Icon

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Virtual Instrumentation** (specifically the LabVIEW graphical programming environment developed by National Instruments), an **Icon** is a graphical, pixel-based symbolic representation of a Virtual Instrument (VI) that appears in the block diagram (as the face of a sub-VI node) and in the upper-right corner of the front panel. It serves as the visual "identity card" of a VI, encapsulating its function, connectivity pattern (connector pane), and intended use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEICT745 ŌĆō Module 1):**
> "Editing and creating an Icon for a VI" is a mandatory learning outcome. Every VI in LabVIEW must have a custom icon before it is used as a sub-VI in a larger hierarchical application. Without a properly defined icon and connector pane, a VI cannot be modularly reused.

The Icon is created/edited using the built-in **Icon Editor** (invoked via *File ŌåÆ VI Properties ŌåÆ General ŌåÆ Edit Icon*, or by double-clicking the default icon at the top-right corner of the Front Panel/Block Diagram).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an Icon exactly like the **logo on the front of a company building** or the **emblem stitched on a uniform pocket**:

- A large company (the calling/top-level VI) hires many smaller departments (sub-VIs) ŌĆö like the HR department, the Accounts department, and the Maintenance department.
- Each department has its **own nameplate/logo** above its door so workers can instantly recognize it.
- Similarly, in LabVIEW, each sub-VI is a "department" performing one specialized task, and the **Icon** is its nameplate.
- If every department used the same generic nameplate (the default LabVIEW cog-wheel icon), the calling VI would be a confusing mess. Custom icons therefore bring **visual clarity, hierarchy, and professionalism** to graphical code.

> [!NOTE]
> **Default Standard:** LabVIEW's default icon area is a **32 ├Ś 32 pixel** monochrome drawing surface (historically). Modern LabVIEW (8.0+) supports up to **256 ├Ś 256 pixel** icons with **32-bit color (BGRA)** and transparency (alpha channel). The Icon Editor also handles the older **16 ├Ś 16 pixel** small-icon variant for compact display in crowded block diagrams.

### 1.3 Key Physical / Logical Constants

- **Small icon dimensions:** 16 ├Ś 16 pixels (rendered when VI is placed as a sub-VI node).
- **Large icon dimensions:** 32 ├Ś 32 pixels (standard display).
- **Maximum editable canvas:** 256 ├Ś 256 pixels (LabVIEW 8.0+).
- **Color depth:** **32-bit BGRA** (8 bits each for Blue, Green, Red, and Alpha transparency).
- **Grid coordinate system:** (0,0) at top-left, (max, max) at bottom-right.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of the Icon Editor

The Icon Editor is a mini image-editor that operates on a **fixed-pixel canvas**. It exposes six logical components:

1. **Tools Palette (left side)**
   - Pencil ŌĆö single-pixel painting.
   - Line, Dropper (eyedropper for color sampling), Fill Bucket, Rectangle, Filled Rectangle, Ellipse, Filled Ellipse, Select (rectangular marquee), Text (A).

2. **Icon Image / Drawing Canvas (center)** ŌĆö the live editing surface.

3. **Template Layer (right side preview)** ŌĆö shows how the icon will look in standard VI contexts.

4. **Layers Pane (right top)**
   - **Foreground Layer (16 ├Ś 16):** for the small icon. Shown only when VI is used in dense block diagrams.
   - **Background Layer (16 ├Ś 16):** filled background for small icon.
   - **Icon (32 ├Ś 32):** primary icon canvas.
   - **Glyphs (32 ├Ś 32):** optional second graphic layer for symbol overlays.

5. **Color Palette (bottom)** ŌĆö 16 default user colors + system transparent/black.

6. **Menus** ŌĆö `Edit` (Undo, Cut, Copy, Paste, Clear), `Layer` (Show/Hide, Import, Export).

### 2.2 Conceptual Breakdown: Why and How Each Step Matters

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Open the Editor:** Right-click the icon pane ŌåÆ *Edit Icon* (or *File ŌåÆ VI Properties*).
  - *Why:* Without a custom icon, every sub-VI looks identical (default gear icon), making hierarchical debugging impossible.

- **Step 2 ŌĆö Choose a Color Scheme:** Use only 16 colors per layer to ensure compatibility with the 256-color display palette used by LabVIEW's runtime.
  - *Why:* Excessive colors produce unpredictable rendering on different OS themes (Windows Classic vs. macOS Aqua).

- **Step 3 ŌĆö Draw the Symbol:** Keep the design abstract and high-contrast. The icon must remain legible at 16 ├Ś 16 pixels.
  - *How:* Use bold, blocky strokes (at least 2 pixels thick) so the icon does not disappear when scaled down.

- **Step 4 ŌĆö Add Text Label:** Write a short identifier (2ŌĆō4 chars) like `DAQ`, `FFT`, `PID`, `I/O`.
  - *Why:* Textual redundancy ensures readability even when graphical detail is lost.

- **Step 5 ŌĆö Set the Connector Pane (Right side):** Although technically separate, an icon without a connector pattern is unusable as a sub-VI. Patterns range from **4-pin** (default) up to **28-pin** layout.

- **Step 6 ŌĆö Save:** Press *OK*. The icon is stored in the `.vi` file binary header.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property | Standard Value | KTU Exam-Ready Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Small icon size | 16 ├Ś 16 px | Used as the sub-VI node appearance |
| Standard icon size | 32 ├Ś 32 px | Primary icon canvas |
| Maximum canvas size | 256 ├Ś 256 px | Available in LabVIEW 8.0+ |
| Color depth | 32-bit (BGRA) | Includes 8-bit alpha channel |
| Layers per icon | 4 (Foreground, Background, Icon, Glyphs) | Each layer editable independently |
| Max connector pins | 28 pins | Configurable via *Patterns* dropdown |
| Default grid unit | 1 pixel | Snap-to-grid is enforced |
| Min text size for legibility | Ōēź 2 px stroke | At 16 ├Ś 16 scale |
| File storage | `.vi` binary header | Icon is embedded, not external |
| Access path | Right-click icon ŌåÆ Edit Icon | Also: `File ŌåÆ VI Properties ŌåÆ General` |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Custom icons are not cosmetic ŌĆö they are **engineering documentation**. In industry, VIs often grow to hundreds of sub-VIs (e.g., a jet-engine test rig may contain 800+ VIs). Custom icons with clear glyphs (thermometer for temperature, sine wave for signal, gear for control) allow engineers to debug visually rather than opening every node. This reduces Mean-Time-To-Repair (MTTR) and is mandated by **NI's style guides** for G-Software certification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Procedure to Create an Icon

Below is the formal, KTU-board-style procedure. Each step is expanded with reasoning and the underlying state transition.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Locate the Icon Pane.**
On any open VI, look at the **upper-right corner** of either the Front Panel or Block Diagram window. A small white rectangular box containing a default gear icon is visible.

> *State transition:* The `.vi` file initially stores an in-memory `IconCluster` whose `Bitmap` field contains the default 32 ├Ś 32 gear pattern.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Invoke the Icon Editor.**
Use one of the following paths:
- (a) Double-click directly on the icon pane.
- (b) Right-click ŌåÆ *Edit Icon* from the context menu.
- (c) `File ŌåÆ VI Properties ŌåÆ Category: General ŌåÆ Edit Icon button`.

> *Result:* A modal dialog window titled **"Icon Editor"** appears, replacing the temporary default bitmap with an editable canvas.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Understand the Layer Architecture.**
The Icon Editor displays four logical layers stacked on top of each other:

$$
\text{Final Icon} = \text{Glyphs Layer} \oplus \text{Icon Layer} \oplus \text{Foreground} \oplus \text{Background}
$$

where $\oplus$ denotes pixel-wise alpha compositing in BGRA color space. Each layer is independently editable.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Select Drawing Tools from the Left Palette.**
Choose tools based on the desired glyph:

- For circular symbols ŌåÆ **Ellipse** tool.
- For rectangular frames ŌåÆ **Rectangle** tool.
- For high-contrast detail ŌåÆ **Pencil** with 2-pixel cursor size.
- For text labels ŌåÆ **Text (A)** tool, then click and type.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply Colors from the Bottom Palette.**
- Click the foreground color swatch ŌåÆ choose the primary symbol color (e.g., **black** or **dark blue**).
- Click the background color swatch ŌåÆ choose the backdrop color (e.g., **white** or **transparent**).

**Step 6 ŌĆö Edit the 16 ├Ś 16 Small Icon.**
Click the **"Foreground (16 ├Ś 16)"** thumbnail. Redraw the icon at a coarser level of detail. Because the canvas is only 256 pixels total, every pixel is significant. Use only blocky, recognizable geometry.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Add Optional Text Label.**
Select the Text tool (A). Click on the canvas and type a 2ŌĆō4 character string such as `TEMP`, `DAQ`, `PWM`. Press *Enter* to commit.

**Step 8 ŌĆö Importing External Graphics (Advanced).**
For complex logos, use *Edit ŌåÆ Import Picture* or drag-drop a `.png` / `.bmp` file. LabVIEW will rescale it to fit the current layer.

> *Constraint equation for scaling:*
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{new\_x} &= \lfloor \text{src\_x} \cdot \frac{32}{\text{src\_width}} \rfloor \\
\text{new\_y} &= \lfloor \text{src\_y} \cdot \frac{32}{\text{src\_height}} \rfloor
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ is the floor function (nearest-neighbor downsampling).

**Step 9 ŌĆö Save the Icon.**
Click **OK** at the bottom-right of the Icon Editor. The bitmap is serialized into the `.vi` file using LabVIEW's proprietary resource format.

**Step 10 ŌĆö Verify the Connector Pane.**
Although not strictly part of "Icon" editing, KTU Module 1 expects students to associate icons with connector patterns. Right-click the small icon pane ŌåÆ *Show Connector* (or the icon next to it). Assign control/indicator terminals to pins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Note (KTU ESE Pattern):**
> A 7-mark question on icon creation typically expects **4 marks for procedural steps** and **3 marks for justification of layer/pixel conventions**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Decision Table for Icon Design

| Scenario | Recommended Foreground | Recommended Background | Layer Count | Text Label |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Data acquisition sub-VI | Dark Blue | White | 2 (Icon + Background) | `DAQ` |
| Signal processing (FFT) | Black | Yellow | 3 (Glyph + Icon + BG) | `FFT` |
| Temperature sensor read | Red | White | 2 | `TEMP` |
| PID controller | Green | White | 3 (with arrow glyph) | `PID` |
| Boolean logic gate | Magenta | Black | 2 | `AND`/`OR` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Icon Creation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Open Target VI] --> B[Locate Icon Pane Top Right Corner]
    B --> C[Right Click and Select Edit Icon]
    C --> D[Icon Editor Window Opens]
    D --> E{Choose Layer}
    E -->|Small 16x16| F1[Foreground Layer]
    E -->|Standard 32x32| F2[Icon Layer]
    E -->|Overlay Symbol| F3[Glyphs Layer]
    F1 --> G[Select Tool from Palette]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Choose Colors from Palette]
    H --> I[Draw Geometry or Add Text Label]
    I --> J[Optional Import Picture from PNG or BMP]
    J --> K[Preview in Template Pane]
    K --> L{Quality Acceptable}
    L -->|No| G
    L -->|Yes| M[Click OK to Save Icon into VI File]
    M --> N[Define Connector Pane Pattern]
    N --> O[VI is Now Ready as Sub VI]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Icon Editor Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Editor[Icon Editor Modal Window]
        T[Tools Palette: Pencil, Line, Fill, Text, Shapes]
        C[Drawing Canvas: 32x32 or 16x16 px]
        L[Layers Pane: Foreground, Background, Icon, Glyphs]
        P[Color Palette: 16 Colors plus Transparent]
        V[Template Preview Pane]
    end
    T --> C
    L --> C
    P --> C
    C --> V
    V -->|OK Click| Out[Serialized Icon Stored in VI Header]
    V -->|Cancel Click| Discard[Discard Changes]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Hierarchy: Icon Use in a Calling VI

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Main[Main Top Level VI Block Diagram]
    Main --> SubA[Sub VI Node showing Icon A: Thermometer Glyph]
    Main --> SubB[Sub VI Node showing Icon B: Sine Wave Glyph]
    Main --> SubC[Sub VI Node showing Icon C: Gear Glyph]
    SubA --> ConnA[Connector Pane: 4 Pins]
    SubB --> ConnB[Connector Pane: 8 Pins]
    SubC --> ConnC[Connector Pane: 12 Pins]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram: Icon Edit Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Default: VI Created
    Default --> EditorOpen: User Double Clicks Icon Pane
    EditorOpen --> Editing: User Draws Pixels
    Editing --> Editing: Undo or Redo
    Editing --> Preview: Layer Switch
    Preview --> Editing: Continue Drawing
    Preview --> Saved: OK Clicked
    Preview --> Discarded: Cancel Clicked
    Discarded --> Default
    Saved --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024 Model]**
*What is an Icon in LabVIEW? State its significance in modular VI development.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An Icon in LabVIEW is a **graphical, pixel-based representation** of a Virtual Instrument that uniquely identifies it as a sub-VI node on a calling VI's block diagram. Its significance lies in (i) providing **visual identification** among hundreds of sub-VIs, (ii) serving as the **anchor for the connector pane** which defines input/output terminals, and (iii) acting as **engineering documentation** that conveys the VI's function at a glance without opening it. A well-designed icon improves code readability and maintainability in large hierarchical applications.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Model]**
*List the default pixel dimensions of the Small and Standard icon layers in LabVIEW.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In LabVIEW:
- **Small icon layer (Foreground):** 16 ├Ś 16 pixels.
- **Standard icon layer (Icon):** 32 ├Ś 32 pixels.
- **Maximum canvas (LabVIEW 8.0+):** 256 ├Ś 256 pixels.

The small icon appears when the VI is used as a sub-VI node on a crowded block diagram, while the standard icon appears in the upper-right corner of the VI's own Front Panel/Block Diagram.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**Q.A. (a)** Explain the architecture of the LabVIEW Icon Editor with the help of a labeled block diagram. Discuss its tool palette, layers, and color palette in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A. (b)** With neat steps, describe the procedure to create a custom icon for a sub-VI representing a Temperature Data Acquisition module. Mention the pixel conventions to be followed. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A.(a):**

The Icon Editor is a modal dialog accessible via *right-click on icon pane ŌåÆ Edit Icon*. Its architecture consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Tools Palette (left vertical strip):** Contains drawing primitives ŌĆö Pencil, Line, Dropper, Fill Bucket, Rectangle, Filled Rectangle, Ellipse, Filled Ellipse, Select, and Text (A). Each tool operates on the currently active layer.

2. **Drawing Canvas (center):** The live pixel grid where the icon is rendered. Default is **32 ├Ś 32 pixels**. Grid snap is always on.

3. **Layers Pane (right top):** Four logical layers exist:
   - **Background (16 ├Ś 16):** Solid backdrop for the small-icon view.
   - **Foreground (16 ├Ś 16):** Detailed small-icon graphic.
   - **Icon (32 ├Ś 32):** Main canvas.
   - **Glyphs (32 ├Ś 32):** Optional overlay (e.g., a transparent logo on top of the icon).

4. **Color Palette (bottom strip):** 16 user-selectable colors + transparent + black.

5. **Template Preview:** Live WYSIWYG preview showing how the icon will appear in a real VI context.

6. **Menu Bar:** `Edit` (Undo/Redo/Cut/Copy/Paste/Clear), `Layer` (Show/Hide, Import from PNG/BMP, Export).

> The output of the editor is a **BGRA bitmap** serialized into the `.vi` file's resource fork.

*(Marks split: 2 for tool palette, 2 for layers, 2 for color/template, 1 for block diagram.)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A.(b):**

**Step 1.** Open the target VI. Locate the icon pane at the upper-right corner of the Front Panel/Block Diagram.

**Step 2.** Double-click the icon pane. The **Icon Editor** dialog opens.

**Step 3.** Click on the **Background (16 ├Ś 16)** layer and select a white color using the color palette. Fill the canvas with the **Fill Bucket** tool.

**Step 4.** Click on the **Icon (32 ├Ś 32)** layer. Select the **Ellipse** tool. Set foreground color to **Red** (representing heat/temperature). Draw a filled ellipse roughly centered, 24 ├Ś 24 pixels in diameter, leaving a 4-pixel white margin.

**Step 5.** Using the **Pencil** tool (2-pixel cursor), draw a vertical line from the bottom of the ellipse to simulate a thermometer bulb stem.

**Step 6.** Click on the **Glyphs (32 ├Ś 32)** layer. Using the **Text (A)** tool, type the label `TEMP` in bold, centered horizontally at the bottom.

**Step 7.** Switch to the **Foreground (16 ├Ś 16)** layer. Redraw a simplified version ŌĆö a single solid red rectangle (representing the thermometer) ŌĆö since fine detail is lost at this scale.

**Step 8.** Click **OK** to commit.

**Pixel conventions followed:**
- Minimum stroke width: **2 pixels** (to survive 16 ├Ś 16 scaling).
- High contrast color pair (red on white).
- Maximum 4-character text label.
- Both 16 ├Ś 16 and 32 ├Ś 32 layers updated for dual-render fidelity.

*(Marks split: 1 for opening editor, 2 for drawing steps, 2 for text/glyph, 2 for pixel conventions.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *Alternative Choice*

**Q.B. (a)** Differentiate between the **Foreground (16 ├Ś 16)** and **Icon (32 ├Ś 32)** layers in the LabVIEW Icon Editor. Why is the 16 ├Ś 16 layer maintained separately? **(7 marks)**

**Q.B. (b)** Explain with a flowchart how a custom icon is embedded into a `.vi` file and later rendered when the VI is called as a sub-VI. Mention the role of the connector pane. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B.(a):**

| Parameter | Foreground (16 ├Ś 16) | Icon (32 ├Ś 32) |
|---|---|---|
| Pixel dimensions | 16 ├Ś 16 | 32 ├Ś 32 |
| Display context | Compact sub-VI node on crowded block diagrams | Upper-right corner of Front Panel/Block Diagram |
| Color limit | 16 colors | 16 colors (per layer) |
| Purpose | Rapid visual recognition at thumbnail scale | Detailed symbolic representation |
| Independent editing | Yes | Yes |

**Why maintain a 16 ├Ś 16 layer separately?**
When a top-level VI contains dozens of sub-VIs, the block diagram must fit on a single screen. Each sub-VI is rendered at a tiny size (effectively 16 ├Ś 16). If only the 32 ├Ś 32 icon existed, LabVIEW would downscale it using nearest-neighbor interpolation, producing blurred, illegible glyphs. By maintaining a hand-tuned 16 ├Ś 16 layer, the designer ensures that **critical visual information (color block, simple shape, short text) survives downsampling**. This is a classic case of **LOD (Level-of-Detail) rendering**, borrowed from computer graphics.

*(Marks split: 3 for table differences, 2 for LOD explanation, 2 for real-world example.)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B.(b):**

```
[User Creates Icon in Editor]
            Ōöé
            Ō¢╝
[Click OK Button]
            Ōöé
            Ō¢╝
[LabVIEW Serializes BGRA Bitmap into VI Resource Header]
            Ōöé
            Ō¢╝
[VI File Saved with Embedded Icon Cluster]
            Ōöé
            Ō¢╝
[VI is Dragged Onto Caller's Block Diagram]
            Ōöé
            Ō¢╝
[LabVIEW Reads 16x16 Layer First]
            Ōöé
            Ō¢╝
[Renders as Sub-VI Node Glyph on Caller's Diagram]
            Ōöé
            Ō¢╝
[Connector Pane Pins Mapped to Terminals of Caller]
            Ōöé
            Ō¢╝
[Data Flows Through Icon Visually Represented]
```

**Role of the Connector Pane:**
While the icon is the visual face, the **connector pane** is the logical interface ŌĆö a rectangular pattern (default 4 pins, expandable up to 28 pins) where inputs (controls) and outputs (indicators) are exposed. When a VI is dropped onto a block diagram, LabVIEW reads both the icon AND the connector pane. Without a connector pane, the icon is purely decorative. Without an icon, the connector pane is unusable. **Both must coexist.**

*(Marks split: 4 for flowchart, 3 for connector pane role.)*

---

### 5.3 ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Icon with Front Panel:** Icons are 32 ├Ś 32 pixel bitmaps, NOT scalable vector UI elements. Writing "icon is the user interface" will fetch 0 marks.
> 2. **Omitting the 16 ├Ś 16 Layer:** KTU examiners frequently test whether students know about the dual-resolution rendering. Simply writing "32 ├Ś 32 icon" loses 2ŌĆō3 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the Connector Pane:** Even if the question says "create an icon," evaluators expect a one-line mention of the connector pane association (7-mark questions deduct 1 mark if omitted).
> 4. **Wrong Units:** Writing "icon is measured in centimeters" or "icon size is 16 ├Ś 16 bits" ŌĆö always state **pixels**.
> 5. **Skipping Justification:** For 7-mark sub-parts, merely listing steps without explaining WHY (e.g., "use 2-pixel strokes so it survives downsampling") yields partial credit only.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ­¤öæ **Definition:** An Icon is a **pixel-based graphical identity** of a LabVIEW VI, typically **32 ├Ś 32 px** standard and **16 ├Ś 16 px** compact.
- ­¤öæ **Editor Access:** Double-click the icon pane at the top-right of Front Panel/Block Diagram, or `File ŌåÆ VI Properties ŌåÆ Edit Icon`.
- ­¤öæ **Four Layers:** Foreground (16├Ś16), Background (16├Ś16), Icon (32├Ś32), Glyphs (32├Ś32).
- ­¤öæ **Color Format:** **32-bit BGRA** including 8-bit alpha transparency.
- ­¤öæ **Tools Palette:** Pencil, Line, Dropper, Fill, Rectangle, Ellipse, Select, Text (A).
- ­¤öæ **Minimum Stroke:** **2 pixels** for legibility after downscaling.
- ­¤öæ **Connector Pane Co-dependency:** An icon without a connector pattern is unusable as a sub-VI. Pin count: default **4**, max **28**.
- ­¤öæ **File Format:** Icon bitmap is embedded inside the `.vi` binary resource header (not an external file).
- ­¤öæ **Engineering Importance:** Custom icons serve as **visual documentation** in large hierarchical VIs (industry standard for code maintenance).
- ­¤öæ **KTU Exam Frequency:** This topic appears in **ESE Module 1** almost every semester ŌĆö usually as a 7-mark sub-question with 3ŌĆō4 marks for procedure and 3ŌĆō4 marks for justification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building a Connector Pane, Displaying SubVIs and Express VIs as Icons or Expandable Nodes, Creating SubVIs from Sections of a VI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT745 - VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Building a Connector Pane, Displaying SubVIs and Express VIs as Icons or Expandable Nodes, Creating SubVIs from Sections of a VI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Connector Pane

The **Connector Pane** is the visual interface in LabVIEW that defines the inputs and outputs of a Virtual Instrument (VI) when it is used as a SubVI. It appears on the **Front Panel** (top-right corner, opposite the Icon) and consists of a set of **terminals** that map to specific Front Panel controls and indicators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A *Connector Pane* is the graphical representation of a VI's Input/Output (I/O) port configuration. Each terminal on the connector pane corresponds to a single Front Panel object (Control or Indicator). It serves as the data-flow handshake protocol when the VI is invoked as a SubVI inside a calling (parent) VI.

**Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The Power Socket Wall:**
Think of the connector pane as the **wall socket** of an appliance. The **icon** is the brand logo of the appliance, while the **connector pane** is the standardized 3-pin socket on its back. When you plug the appliance into a multi-socket extension board (the calling VI's block diagram), electricity (data) flows in through the Live/Neutral pins (input terminals) and out through the Earth pin (output terminal). The appliance itself is a "black box" ŌĆö you don't care how it works inside, only which pins give what. This is exactly how a SubVI behaves: the connector pane defines *what goes in* and *what comes out*.

> [!NOTE]
> The number of terminals is determined by the **Connector Pane Pattern** (e.g., 4├Ś2├Ś2├Ś4, 8├Ś8, 5├Ś4├Ś3├Ś2). LabVIEW automatically selects a default pattern based on the number of front panel objects. The pattern is divided into two halves: the **left half** conventionally hosts **Input terminals** (controls) and the **right half** hosts **Output terminals** (indicators).

---

### 1.2 SubVIs and Express VIs ŌĆö Icons vs. Expandable Nodes

A **SubVI** is a VI that has been incorporated into the block diagram of another VI, analogous to a *subroutine* in text-based programming. An **Express VI** is a specialized, interactive SubVI that typically requires minimal wiring because it uses a configuration dialog box.

When a SubVI or Express VI is placed on a block diagram, it can be displayed in **two visual modes**:

| Display Mode | Visual Appearance | Engineering Use-Case |
|--------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Icon** (Default) | Compact 32├Ś32 pixel box with a graphical icon | Saves block diagram space, used in finalized hierarchical VIs |
| **Expandable Node** | Full rectangular shell showing every input/output terminal as individual wires | Used during debugging, learning, or when many terminals need to be wired without using a constant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The selection between Icon and Expandable Node is purely **cosmetic** ŌĆö it does not alter the VI's functional behaviour, data type handling, or execution priority. The choice is made by right-clicking the SubVI node and selecting **"View As Icon"** (toggle).

**Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The Function Signature:**
In C programming, a function prototype `double sqrt(double x)` is compact and abstract. But when you expand it during debugging, you see the actual assembly registers (ST0, EAX, etc.) and every intermediate value. **Icon mode** is the compact prototype; **Expandable Node mode** is the fully unwrapped register view.

---

### 1.3 Creating SubVIs from Sections of a VI

A SubVI can be created from a *portion* of an existing block diagram. This is a powerful modularization technique that:
- Reduces block diagram clutter
- Encapsulates reusable logic
- Improves maintainability and readability

> [!NOTE]
> The standard KTU 2024 procedure involves **selection ŌåÆ Edit ŌåÆ Create SubVI**. LabVIEW automatically generates the connector pane, assigns input terminals for incoming wires, and output terminals for outgoing wires, based on the direction of data flow.

**Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The Recipe Card:**
Imagine a 10-page recipe book. You notice that "Make Dough" is repeated in 4 different recipes. You tear those pages out, paste them onto a single card called **"Make Dough"**, punch a hole in the corner, and clip it onto a ring. Now, every recipe just writes *"See card 3"* instead of rewriting the entire 2 pages. That clipped card is your **SubVI**, and the punch hole is your **connector pane**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Workflow ŌĆö Building a Connector Pane

The process of building a connector pane follows a strict sequence of operations defined by National Instruments (NI) LabVIEW design standards:

1. **Open the Front Panel** of the target VI.
2. **Define the Controls and Indicators** that the SubVI must expose to the outside world.
3. **Right-click the Icon pane** (top-right corner of Front Panel) and select **"Show Connector"** from the context menu.
4. LabVIEW automatically selects a connector pattern based on the count of front panel objects.
5. If the auto-selected pattern is inappropriate, right-click the connector pane and select **"Patterns"** ŌåÆ choose from the 28 predefined patterns.
6. **Click an unused terminal** on the connector pane ŌĆö it turns black.
7. **Click the corresponding Front Panel control or indicator** to wire it to the selected terminal (the terminal becomes coloured to match the data type).
8. **Repeat steps 6ŌĆō7** until every required I/O is mapped.
9. (Optional) Right-click terminal ŌåÆ **"This Connection Is"** ŌåÆ set as **Required**, **Recommended**, or **Optional**. This affects the appearance of the input on the calling VI's block diagram.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A common 7-mark question asks *"List the steps to build a connector pane for a SubVI."* Marks are awarded specifically for mentioning the **Patterns menu**, the **colour-coding by data type**, and the **"This Connection Is"** input/terminal settings.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Table

| S.No | Concept | Standard LabVIEW Definition | Engineering Significance |
|:----:|---------|------------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Connector Pane** | Visual I/O port map of a SubVI | Defines subVI's API contract |
| 2 | **Terminal** | Single port on the connector pane | Maps to one Front Panel object |
| 3 | **Pattern** | Predefined arrangement of terminals | Determines max $\vert \text{I/O} \vert$ capacity |
| 4 | **Required Terminal** | Bold-recommended border; must be wired | Enforces compile-time safety |
| 5 | **Optional Terminal** | Dashed border; can be left unwired | Provides default-value flexibility |
| 6 | **Icon Mode** | Compact 32├Ś32 node representation | Saves graphical real-estate |
| 7 | **Expandable Node** | Unwrapped rectangular shell with terminals | Aids debugging and learning |
| 8 | **Express VI** | Configurable SubVI with dialog box | Reduces wiring via dynamic configuration |
| 9 | **Standard SubVI** | Pure data-flow VIs wired manually | Maximum flexibility and control |
| 10 | **Create SubVI** | `Edit ŌåÆ Create SubVI` selection-based command | Modularization engine of LabVIEW |

> [!NOTE]
> In LabVIEW, a connector pattern is mathematically described as a tuple $(m, n)$ where $m$ and $n$ denote the number of terminals in the left (input) and right (output) halves respectively. Common KTU-tested patterns include $(2,2)$, $(4,4)$, and the complex $4 \times 2 \times 2 \times 4$ layout (28 terminals total).

---

### 2.3 Comparison: Icon Mode vs. Expandable Node Mode

| Parameter | Icon Mode | Expandable Node Mode |
|-----------|-----------|----------------------|
| **Visual Footprint** | Minimal ($\approx 32 \times 32$ px) | Large (proportional to terminal count) |
| **Terminal Visibility** | Hidden; access via **Wiring tool** | Every terminal displayed as a separate pin |
| **Code Readability** | Low (must open SubVI to inspect) | High (all I/O visible at a glance) |
| **Use During Development** | Less preferred | Preferred for debugging |
| **Use in Finalized Code** | Preferred | Less preferred (saves space) |
| **Memory Footprint** | Identical | Identical |
| **Execution Speed** | Identical | Identical |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | High (often confused with "node" vs "icon" VIs) | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Express VIs appear as **blue-colored icons with a blue field**, whereas standard SubVIs appear as **yellow field icons**. This colour-coding is a frequent KTU 2-mark question. The display mode (Icon vs. Expandable) is *orthogonal* to the VI type (Express vs Standard) ŌĆö both can be toggled.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Modular Test Systems**: In industrial DAQ (Data Acquisition) systems, a SubVI titled "Read Temperature" with a connector pane having 2 inputs (channel number, averaging filter) and 1 output (temperature value) can be reused across hundreds of test stations.
- **Signal Processing Pipelines**: An Express VI "Filter" is used as an expandable node during algorithm development, then converted to icon mode for the final production VI to reduce block diagram clutter.
- **Hierarchical VIs**: Top-level Test Executive VIs invoke low-level SubVIs exclusively in icon mode to keep the block diagram compact and presentation-ready.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Procedure A ŌĆö Building a Connector Pane (Exhaustive)

**Scenario:** You have a VI named `Compute_Stats.vi` that calculates the **mean** and **standard deviation** of an input array. It has 3 Front Panel objects: `Input Array` (Control), `Mean` (Indicator), `StdDev` (Indicator). You want to convert this VI into a reusable SubVI.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Front Panel Verification**

Confirm that all 3 controls/indicators exist on the Front Panel. In KTU evaluations, students often lose marks for missing controls/indicators on the front panel *before* building the connector pane.

$$
\text{Front Panel Objects} = \big\{ \text{Control}: \text{Input\_Array}, \; \text{Indicator}: \text{Mean}, \; \text{Indicator}: \text{StdDev} \big\}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Show the Connector**

Right-click the Icon pane (currently showing the default LabVIEW logo) and select **"Show Connector"** from the context menu. The icon area is replaced by a connector pattern.

$$
\text{Icon} \xrightarrow{\text{Right-Click}} \text{Show Connector} \Rightarrow \text{Connector Pane Activated}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Pattern Auto-Selection**

Since there are 3 front panel objects, LabVIEW auto-selects a pattern with at least 3 terminals. The most common default is a $2 \times 2$ pattern (4 terminals total, 2 left, 2 right).

$$
N_{\text{terminals}} = \max(N_{\text{inputs}}, N_{\text{outputs}}) + \text{reserved slack}
$$

For our case, we have 1 input and 2 outputs, so LabVIEW selects a pattern that supports $1 \text{ input}$ and $2 \text{ outputs}$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Wire the First Terminal (Input Array)**

- Using the **Wiring tool** (automatic icon: spool of wire), click the **top-left terminal** of the connector pane. It turns black (selected).
- Click the `Input Array` control on the Front Panel. The terminal becomes **orange** (matches the data type of a 1D array of double-precision floats: **DBL**).

$$
\text{Terminal}_{\text{TL}} \rightarrow \text{Control: Input\_Array} \Rightarrow \text{Colour: Orange (DBL Array)}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Wire the Second Terminal (Mean Indicator)**

- Click the **top-right terminal** of the connector pane.
- Click the `Mean` indicator. The terminal becomes orange.

$$
\text{Terminal}_{\text{TR}} \rightarrow \text{Indicator: Mean} \Rightarrow \text{Colour: Orange (DBL Scalar)}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Wire the Third Terminal (StdDev Indicator)**

- Click the **bottom-right terminal** of the connector pane.
- Click the `StdDev` indicator. The terminal becomes orange.

$$
\text{Terminal}_{\text{BR}} \rightarrow \text{Indicator: StdDev} \Rightarrow \text{Colour: Orange (DBL Scalar)}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Set Terminal Properties (Optional but Recommended for Marks)**

Right-click each terminal and inspect **"This Connection Is"**:

- `Input Array` ŌåÆ **Required** (bold outline)
- `Mean` ŌåÆ **Required** (bold outline)
- `StdDev` ŌåÆ **Optional** (dashed outline, default 0.0 if unwired)

$$
\text{Terminal State} = \big\{ \text{Required}, \; \text{Recommended}, \; \text{Optional} \big\}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Setting an output terminal to **"Recommended"** or **"Optional"** is uncommon; outputs are typically **Required**. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes this distinction.

**Step 8 ŌĆö Save and Test**

Save the VI as `Compute_Stats.vi`. It is now ready to be dropped into any other VI's block diagram as a SubVI.

---

### 3.2 Procedure B ŌĆö Displaying SubVIs/Express VIs as Icons or Expandable Nodes

**Step 1 ŌĆö Locate the SubVI Node**

On the block diagram of the calling VI, identify the SubVI (yellow icon) or Express VI (blue icon) you wish to toggle.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Right-Click the Node**

Right-click the SubVI icon to bring up the context menu.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Select "View As Icon"**

The **"View As Icon"** option has a checkmark (Ō£ō) next to it in Icon mode. Click it to **toggle** between Icon mode and Expandable Node mode.

$$
\text{View Mode} = \big\{ \text{Icon Mode (compact)}, \; \text{Expandable Node (expanded)} \big\}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Verify the Result**

In Expandable Node mode, the node appears as a wide rectangular shell with each input and output displayed as a separate pin, even those not currently wired.

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU 2-mark question: *"What is the default display mode for a newly placed SubVI?"* **Answer:** Icon mode. The default size is **$32 \times 32$ pixels**.

---

### 3.3 Procedure C ŌĆö Creating a SubVI from a Section of a VI

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the Reusable Section**

On the block diagram, locate the section of code (a group of nodes and wires) that performs a logically complete operation.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Select the Section**

Using the **Positioning tool** (arrow), click-and-drag a selection rectangle around the target code. Ensure that all incoming wires (inputs) and outgoing wires (outputs) are fully included.

$$
\text{Selected Region} = \big\{ \text{Node}_1, \text{Node}_2, \ldots, \text{Node}_n, \; \text{Wires within bounds} \big\}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Invoke the Create SubVI Command**

From the menu bar, navigate to **Edit ŌåÆ Create SubVI**.

$$
\text{Edit} \rightarrow \text{Create SubVI}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Automatic Generation**

LabVIEW automatically:
- Replaces the selected section with a **newly generated SubVI icon**.
- Creates a **connector pane** with terminals corresponding to:
  - **Input terminals** for wires entering the selected region.
  - **Output terminals** for wires leaving the selected region.
- The default SubVI name is `SubVI.vi` (must be renamed and saved).

**Step 5 ŌĆö Customize the New SubVI**

Double-click the new SubVI icon to open it. The selected code is now inside a fresh VI. Customize:
- Front Panel (add controls/indicators if needed).
- Icon (right-click ŌåÆ Edit Icon, draw a meaningful graphical symbol).
- Connector pane (add/remove terminals, set Required/Optional).
- File ŌåÆ Save As ŌåÆ `MyFunction.vi`.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Replace in Calling VI**

The calling VI's block diagram now shows the new SubVI in place of the original code. Wire the existing inputs to the new terminals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** Always verify that the **error clusters** (standard LabVIEW error in/out) are connected properly when creating SubVIs. A broken error wire inside a SubVI causes the entire calling hierarchy to fail silently. KTU examiners frequently test this.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Error-Handling Pattern

| Error Scenario | Symptom | KTU-Accepted Fix |
|----------------|---------|------------------|
| Connector pane has no terminals | SubVI shows as a yellow icon with no inputs | Right-click Icon ŌåÆ Show Connector ŌåÆ repeat Procedure A |
| Wrong pattern selected | Not enough terminals for all controls/indicators | Right-click Connector ŌåÆ Patterns ŌåÆ choose a larger layout |
| Terminal colour mismatches data type | Wire shows broken (dashed) | Re-wire to a terminal matching the data type |
| SubVI displays as expandable but you need compact | Block diagram is too cluttered | Right-click ŌåÆ View As Icon (enable) |
| `Create SubVI` produces broken wires | The original code had dynamic subVIs or other SubVIs that don't allow re-entrancy | Manually create the SubVI using **File ŌåÆ New VI** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture ŌĆö SubVI Creation Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete data-flow when a section of code is converted into a SubVI and then invoked from a parent VI.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original VI Block Diagram] --> B[Identify Reusable Code Section]
    B --> C[Select Code with Positioning Tool]
    C --> D[Edit Menu - Create SubVI]
    D --> E{LabVIEW Engine Analysis}
    E -->|Wires Entering Selection| F[Assign Input Terminals]
    E -->|Wires Leaving Selection| G[Assign Output Terminals]
    F --> H[New SubVI Front Panel]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Edit Icon and Connector Pane]
    I --> J[Save SubVI to File]
    J --> K[Parent VI Block Diagram Updated]
    K --> L{Display Mode Choice}
    L -->|Icon Mode| M[Compact 32x32 Yellow Icon]
    L -->|Expandable Node| N[Rectangular Shell with All Terminals Visible]
    M --> O[Calling VI Executes SubVI]
    N --> O
```

---

### 4.2 Decision Tree ŌĆö Icon vs. Expandable Node Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start: Place SubVI on Block Diagram] --> Q{Is this for final production code?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Select Icon Mode]
    Q -->|No| S{Is debugging or learning in progress?}
    S -->|Yes| T[Select Expandable Node]
    S -->|No| U{Does the SubVI have more than 4 terminals?}
    U -->|Yes| V[Consider Expandable Node for clarity]
    U -->|No| W[Default Icon Mode is sufficient]
    R --> X[Final VIs are compact and presentation-ready]
    T --> Y[All I/O visible at a glance]
    V --> Y
    W --> X
```

---

### 4.3 Connector Pane Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FRONTPANEL [Front Panel of SubVI]
        CTRL1[Input Control 1]
        CTRL2[Input Control 2]
        IND1[Output Indicator 1]
        IND2[Output Indicator 2]
    end
    subgraph CONN [Connector Pane 2x2 Pattern]
        T1((Terminal TL - Input))
        T2((Terminal TR - Input))
        T3((Terminal BL - Output))
        T4((Terminal BR - Output))
    end
    CTRL1 -.-> T1
    CTRL2 -.-> T2
    IND1 -.-> T3
    IND2 -.-> T4
    CONN ==> CALLING[Calling VI Block Diagram]
    CALLING ==> EXEC[Execution with proper data routing]
```

---

### 4.4 SubVI Modularization Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Top Level Test Executive VI] --> SUB1[SubVI - Acquire Signal]
    ROOT --> SUB2[SubVI - Filter Signal]
    ROOT --> SUB3[SubVI - Compute Statistics]
    SUB1 --> S1A[Channel Number Input]
    SUB1 --> S1B[Samples Output]
    SUB2 --> S2A[Raw Signal Input]
    SUB2 --> S2B[Filtered Output]
    SUB3 --> S3A[Data Array Input]
    SUB3 --> S3B[Mean Output]
    SUB3 --> S3C[StdDev Output]
    ROOT -.->|All SubVIs shown in Icon Mode| CLUTTERFREE[Clean, Hierarchical Block Diagram]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
*Define a connector pane in LabVIEW. Mention any two of its key properties.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A connector pane is the visual I/O port representation of a VI when it is used as a SubVI. It appears on the top-right corner of the Front Panel, opposite the Icon.
**Key Properties (any two):**
1. It consists of terminals that correspond to Front Panel controls and indicators.
2. Terminals are colour-coded according to the data type (e.g., orange for double-precision, blue for integers, green for booleans).
3. Each terminal can be set as **Required**, **Recommended**, or **Optional**, affecting its appearance on the calling VI.
4. LabVIEW provides 28 predefined patterns (e.g., $4 \times 2 \times 2 \times 4$, $8 \times 8$, $2 \times 2$) that can be selected via the **Patterns** submenu.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023]**
*Differentiate between displaying a SubVI as an icon and as an expandable node.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Icon Mode | Expandable Node Mode |
|--------|-----------|----------------------|
| **Appearance** | Compact 32├Ś32 pixel box with a graphical icon | Wide rectangular shell with individual terminal pins visible |
| **Use-Case** | Final production code, presentation | Debugging, learning, complex VIs with many terminals |
| **Space** | Minimal block diagram footprint | Larger block diagram footprint |
| **Visibility of Terminals** | Hidden inside the icon | All input/output pins shown explicitly |

The choice is toggled by right-clicking the SubVI node and selecting **"View As Icon"** (it has a checkmark in Icon mode). The functional behaviour remains identical in both modes.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a step-by-step procedure how a connector pane is built in LabVIEW. State the significance of the **"This Connection Is"** option.

**(b) [7 Marks)** With a neat block diagram, describe the process of creating a SubVI from a section of an existing VI. What happens automatically to the wires entering and leaving the selected region?

##### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Step 1:** Open the Front Panel of the target VI. Ensure all required controls and indicators are present.

**Step 2:** Right-click the Icon pane (top-right corner of the Front Panel). Select **"Show Connector"** from the context menu. The Icon is replaced by a connector pattern.

**Step 3:** LabVIEW auto-selects a connector pattern based on the number of Front Panel objects. To change the pattern, right-click the connector pane ŌåÆ **Patterns** ŌåÆ choose a suitable layout (e.g., $2 \times 2$, $4 \times 4$, etc.).

**Step 4:** Using the **Wiring tool**, click an unused terminal on the connector pane. The terminal turns black, indicating it is selected.

**Step 5:** Click the corresponding Front Panel control or indicator. The terminal changes colour to match the data type of the object. Repeat for every I/O.

**Step 6:** Right-click each wired terminal and select **"This Connection Is"**. Set the terminal as:
- **Required:** The input must be wired when the SubVI is called (bold outline on the calling VI).
- **Recommended:** Wiring is encouraged but not enforced (normal outline).
- **Optional:** Wiring is optional; a default value is used if left unwired (dashed outline).

**Significance:** This setting enforces compile-time safety and provides visual cues to other developers about which inputs/outputs are critical for the SubVI's operation. Required inputs appear with **bold** wiring on the calling VI's block diagram, making them visually distinct.

##### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Procedure to create a SubVI from a section:**

1. On the block diagram, identify the reusable section of code.
2. Using the **Positioning tool** (arrow cursor), drag a selection rectangle to enclose the target nodes and all relevant wires.
3. From the menu bar, select **Edit ŌåÆ Create SubVI**.
4. LabVIEW automatically replaces the selected region with a new SubVI icon (default name: `SubVI.vi`).
5. The wires that **entered** the selected region are mapped to **input terminals** on the SubVI's connector pane.
6. The wires that **left** the selected region are mapped to **output terminals** on the SubVI's connector pane.
7. Double-click the new SubVI to open it. Customize the Front Panel, Icon, and Connector Pane.
8. Save the new VI with a meaningful name.

**Automatic wire handling:** LabVIEW scans the boundary of the selection rectangle. Each wire that crosses the boundary from outside to inside becomes an **input terminal**; each wire that crosses from inside to outside becomes an **output terminal**. The SubVI's connector pane is generated with these terminals, and the original code is encapsulated inside the new VI.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is the difference between a SubVI and an Express VI in LabVIEW? Explain how Express VIs can be displayed as expandable nodes.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the use of the **Icon** and **Connector Pane** of a VI. Why is the connector pane essential when converting a VI to a SubVI?

##### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Difference between SubVI and Express VI:**

| Parameter | Standard SubVI | Express VI |
|-----------|----------------|------------|
| **Colour** | Yellow field | Blue field |
| **Configuration** | Manual wiring of inputs/outputs | Interactive configuration dialog box |
| **Customization** | Fully customizable code | Limited to the parameters exposed by the dialog |
| **Learning Curve** | Steeper (requires G programming knowledge) | Gentler (suitable for beginners) |
| **Use-Case** | Production-grade, complex applications | Rapid prototyping, simple tasks |

**Express VIs as Expandable Nodes:**
Express VIs are placed on the block diagram with a default Icon-mode appearance (compact blue box). To display an Express VI as an expandable node, right-click it and deselect **"View As Icon"**. The node then expands to a rectangular shell showing all the input and output terminals, including those not currently wired. This is useful for:
- Understanding the Express VI's data flow
- Wiring additional optional inputs without opening the configuration dialog
- Debugging data type mismatches

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students confuse **"Express VI"** with **"Expandable Node"**. They are **unrelated concepts**. An Express VI is a *type* of VI (configurable, blue); an expandable node is a *display mode* applicable to **any** VI (Standard or Express). Losing 2 marks for this confusion is common.

##### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Use of the Icon:**
The Icon is a small graphical representation of the VI, typically a 32├Ś32 pixel image. It serves to:
- Visually identify the VI on a block diagram.
- Represent the VI's purpose (e.g., a thermometer icon for a temperature-reading SubVI).
- Be edited using the **Icon Editor** (right-click Icon ŌåÆ Edit Icon).

**Use of the Connector Pane:**
The connector pane defines the **Input/Output port configuration** of the VI. It serves to:
- Map Front Panel controls/indicators to input/output terminals.
- Allow the VI to receive data (inputs) and return results (outputs) when called as a SubVI.
- Enforce data type consistency through colour-coding.

**Why the Connector Pane is Essential for SubVI Conversion:**
Without a connector pane, a SubVI has no defined I/O interface. The calling VI would have no way to pass data into the SubVI or receive results back. The connector pane is the **API contract** of the SubVI ŌĆö it specifies *what goes in* and *what comes out*. Without it, the SubVI is a closed black box that cannot participate in dataflow programming.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas (Compiled from KTU Valuation Reports):**
> 1. **Confusing SubVI with Subroutine:** A SubVI is *not* a textual subroutine. It is a full VI with its own Front Panel, Block Diagram, Icon, and Connector Pane. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the Icon-Connector Pair:** Some students describe only the Icon or only the Connector Pane. The question asks for **both** in many cases. (-2 marks)
> 3. **Wrong Default Pattern:** KTU students often claim the default connector pattern is $4 \times 4$. It is **not** ŌĆö LabVIEW auto-selects based on object count. The $4 \times 2 \times 2 \times 4$ pattern is one of the largest, not the default. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Skipping the Save step:** Marks are deducted if the procedure does not end with `File ŌåÆ Save`. (-1 mark)
> 5. **Mixing up "Required" and "Optional":** Required inputs have **bold** borders on the calling VI; Optional inputs have **dashed** borders. Getting this reversed loses 1ŌĆō2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Connector Pane** = the **I/O port map** of a SubVI; resides on the Front Panel.
- **Icon** = the **visual identifier** of a VI; resides on the Front Panel.
- **Icon Mode** = compact 32├Ś32 pixel representation of a SubVI/Express VI on a block diagram.
- **Expandable Node Mode** = full rectangular shell showing every terminal of the SubVI/Express VI.
- **Express VI** = blue-colored, dialog-configured SubVI; **Standard SubVI** = yellow-colored, manually-wired SubVI.
- **Create SubVI** command automatically generates the connector pane from boundary-crossing wires.
- **"This Connection Is"** options: **Required** (bold), **Recommended** (normal), **Optional** (dashed).
- Terminals are **colour-coded by data type** (orange = DBL, blue = I32, green = Boolean, brown = cluster, pink = string).
- LabVIEW provides **28 predefined connector patterns**; common ones include $2 \times 2$, $4 \times 4$, and $4 \times 2 \times 2 \times 4$.
- A SubVI is essentially a **black box** with a defined I/O API, promoting modularity and code reuse.
- Always include **error clusters** in SubVI connector panes for robust error handling.
- The `Edit ŌåÆ Create SubVI` workflow is the most efficient way to modularize large block diagrams.
- For KTU practical exams, **saving the VI with a meaningful filename** is mandatory; default names like `SubVI.vi` cause mark deductions.
- **Icon** and **Connector Pane** are toggled by right-clicking the Front Panel's top-right corner (default: Icon is shown, connector is hidden).
- Selecting **"View As Icon"** in the block diagram context menu toggles between the two display modes.
- SubVIs can be **nested** indefinitely (a SubVI can call other SubVIs), enabling hierarchical VIs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Opening and Editing SubVIs, Placing SubVIs on Block Diagrams, Saving SubVIs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT745 - VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Opening and Editing SubVIs, Placing SubVIs on Block Diagrams, Saving SubVIs # ­¤ōś MODULE 1 ŌĆö SubVIs in LabVIEW: Opening, Editing, Placing, and Saving ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **SubVI (Sub Virtual Instrument)** in LabVIEW is a stand-alone, modular, and reusable VI that is incorporated into the **block diagram** of a higher-level (calling) VI. Functionally, it is the **graphical equivalent of a subroutine, function, or method** in textual programming languages (such as a `function()` in C or a `def` in Python). A SubVI consists of a **Front Panel**, a **Block Diagram**, an **Icon**, and a **Connector Pane** that defines its input/output terminals ŌĆö making it a fully encapsulated, self-contained computational unit. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** As per the PEICT745 syllabus, mastering SubVIs is fundamental to achieving **hierarchical** and **modular programming** in LabVIEW, which is the cornerstone of professional Virtual Instrumentation design. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Explanation Imagine you are building a complex machine out of **LEGO blocks**. Instead of carving every gear, axle, and panel from scratch, you build smaller sub-assemblies first (a wheel, a chassis, a motor unit), and then click them together to form the final robot. In LabVIEW: - The **LEGO brick** = a **SubVI** (a pre-built mini-program) - The **final robot** = the **Top-Level VI** (your main application) - The **clicking points** on the brick = the **Connector Pane terminals** - The **sticker on top of the brick** = the **Icon** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why SubVIs matter in engineering:** A well-designed SubVI promotes **code reuse**, **readability**, **scalability**, and **debugging efficiency**. Instead of repeating the same block-diagram code 10 times in your main VI, you build it ONCE as a SubVI and call it 10 times ŌĆö just like calling a function in C. ### 1.3 Relationship Between VIs and SubVIs > [!NOTE] > In LabVIEW, **there is no difference between a VI and a SubVI**. The same file can be the "top-level VI" when opened directly, and a "SubVI" when placed inside another VI's block diagram. The distinction is purely *contextual* based on how the VI is being used. | Aspect | Top-Level VI (Main VI) | SubVI | |---|---|---| | Execution | Run by user (Run button) | Called from another VI's block diagram | | File Extension | `.vi` | `.vi` | | Icon & Connector | Has both | Has both | | Hierarchy | Root of the call chain | A node in the call chain | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Anatomical Parts of a SubVI Every SubVI that you can place on a block diagram must have three prepared components: ### ­¤¤” Part A ŌĆö The Icon - A **graphical representation** (small picture) of the SubVI. - Visible in the **upper-right corner of the Front Panel** and the **upper-left corner of the Block Diagram** of the SubVI itself. - Appears as the **icon of the node** when the SubVI is placed on another VI's block diagram. - Can be edited using the **Icon Editor** (`Right-Click on Icon ŌåÆ Edit Icon`). ### ­¤¤® Part B ŌĆö The Connector Pane - A **set of terminals** (input/output ports) on the SubVI's Front Panel. - Each terminal corresponds to a **Control** (input) or **Indicator** (output) on the SubVI's Front Panel. - Acts as the **data-flow interface** between the calling VI and the SubVI. - Pattern is configurable (e.g., 4├Ś2├Ś2├Ś4, 2├Ś2, etc.). ### ­¤¤© Part C ŌĆö The Block Diagram (the actual logic) - The graphical code that performs the SubVI's function. - Wired to the **Connector Pane terminals** to receive inputs and return outputs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** Without an **Icon** AND a **Connector Pane**, the VI CANNOT be used as a SubVI on another block diagram. The connector pane is what makes data flow IN and OUT. ## 2.2 KTU Reference Cheat Sheet ŌĆö SubVI Properties | Property / Concept | Specification | KTU Exam Importance | |---|---|---| | File Extension | **`.vi`** | ŌŁÉ High (frequently asked) | | Required Components | Icon + Connector Pane + Block Diagram + Front Panel | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ Highest | | Max Inputs (typical default) | **28 terminals** (configurable up to 28) | ŌŁÉ Medium | | Recommended SubVI Inputs | Ōēż **16 inputs** (NI best-practice) | ŌŁÉ Medium | | Default Connector Pane Pattern | **4 ├Ś 2 ├Ś 2 ├Ś 4** (12 terminals) | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ High | | SubVI Shortcut for VI Hierarchy | `File ŌåÆ Open` or `Project Explorer` | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ High | | SubVI Call Style | `SubVI Node` (rectangular block on block diagram) | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ High | | `Show Connector` Shortcut | `View ŌåÆ Show Connector` | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ High | | Inline vs. Non-inline SubVI | Right-click node ŌåÆ **SubVI Node Setup** | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ High | | Saving Format | `File ŌåÆ Save` (as `.vi`) | ŌŁÉ Highest | ## 2.3 Why Use SubVIs? ŌĆö The Four Pillars of Modular Design 1. **Modularity** ŌĆö Break large problems into smaller, manageable blocks. 2. **Reusability** ŌĆö Build once, use in many VIs (e.g., a temperature-conversion SubVI). 3. **Readability** ŌĆö Block diagrams with SubVI nodes are cleaner and self-documenting. 4. **Maintainability & Debugging** ŌĆö Each SubVI can be tested independently with **Highlight Execution** and **Probes**. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In industrial automation (e.g., a SCADA system for a power plant), a SubVI named `Read_Analog_Channel.vi` can be called 100+ times across multiple VIs to acquire data from 100 sensors ŌĆö saving months of development time. # 3. Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 PROCEDURE 1 ŌĆö Opening a SubVI for Editing There are **three primary methods** to open a SubVI in LabVIEW: ### Method 1: Double-Click the SubVI Node 1. On the calling VI's **block diagram**, locate the **SubVI node** (the rectangular icon). 2. **Double-click** the node. 3. LabVIEW opens the SubVI's **Front Panel** in a new window. 4. Switch to the **Block Diagram** (`Ctrl + E`) to view/modify the code. 5. Edit the logic, icon, or connector pane as needed. 6. Save the SubVI (`Ctrl + S`). ### Method 2: Right-Click ŌåÆ Open 1. On the calling VI's block diagram, **right-click** the SubVI node. 2. From the context menu, select **`Open`** (or **`Open Front Panel`**). 3. The SubVI opens for editing in a new window. ### Method 3: Project Explorer 1. Open the **Project Explorer** (`Project ŌåÆ Project Files`). 2. Navigate to the SubVI file in the project tree. 3. **Double-click** the filename. 4. The SubVI opens in the LabVIEW workspace. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** When you edit a SubVI, the change is **propagated to ALL calling VIs** automatically the next time they are loaded, because they reference the same `.vi` file on disk. This is the magic of modularity. ## 3.2 PROCEDURE 2 ŌĆö Editing a SubVI Editing operations include modifying the block diagram, the front panel, the **icon**, and the **connector pane**. ### Step-by-Step: Editing the Icon 1. Open the SubVI's Front Panel. 2. In the **upper-right corner**, **double-click the icon** (or right-click ŌåÆ `Edit Icon`). 3. The **Icon Editor** dialog appears. 4. Use the drawing tools (line, rectangle, text) to design the icon. 5. Click **OK** to apply. ### Step-by-Step: Editing the Connector Pane 1. Open the SubVI's Front Panel. 2. Right-click the **icon** in the upper-right corner ŌåÆ select **`Show Connector`**. 3. The icon transforms into a **grid of small squares** (the connector pane pattern). 4. **Click a terminal** on the connector pane ŌĆö it turns black. 5. **Click a control or indicator** on the Front Panel ŌĆö the terminal is now bound to it. 6. Repeat for all desired controls (inputs) and indicators (outputs). 7. Right-click the connector ŌåÆ **`Patterns`** to change the layout (e.g., 2├Ś2, 4├Ś2├Ś2├Ś4). ### Step-by-Step: Editing the Block Diagram Logic 1. With the SubVI open, press **`Ctrl + E`** to switch to the Block Diagram window. 2. Add, remove, or wire functions, structures, and wires. 3. Confirm all input controls and output indicators are **wired to the connector pane terminals**. 4. Save (`Ctrl + S`). ## 3.3 PROCEDURE 3 ŌĆö Placing a SubVI on a Block Diagram There are **three methods** to place a SubVI onto a calling VI's block diagram: ### Method 1: Drag-and-Drop from File System 1. Open **Windows File Explorer** (or Finder on macOS). 2. Locate the `.vi` file of the SubVI. 3. **Drag the file** into the target VI's Block Diagram window. 4. The SubVI node is dropped at the cursor position. ### Method 2: `Select a VI` Palette Function 1. Open the target VI's **Block Diagram**. 2. Right-click an empty area to open the **Functions Palette**. 3. Navigate to **`Select a VI...`** (usually under the user libraries or the top-level palette). 4. Browse to the SubVI `.vi` file and click **OK**. 5. The SubVI node appears on the cursor ŌĆö click to place it. ### Method 3: Project Explorer Drag 1. Open the **Project Explorer** window. 2. Locate the SubVI in the project tree. 3. **Drag** the SubVI from the project pane into the Block Diagram of the target VI. 4. The node is placed; wire its terminals to the rest of the block diagram. > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** Once placed, you can wire to the SubVI's **input terminals (left side)** and **output terminals (right side)** just like any other LabVIEW function node. Required (non-default) inputs must be wired; recommended inputs may be left unwired. ## 3.4 PROCEDURE 4 ŌĆö Saving a SubVI ### Method 1: Standard Save 1. With the SubVI window active, press **`Ctrl + S`**. 2. If the file has never been saved, a **Save As** dialog appears. 3. Specify the **filename** (e.g., `Temperature_Convert.vi`) and **directory**. 4. Click **Save**. ### Method 2: Save As (Save a Copy / Rename) 1. Select **`File ŌåÆ Save As...`** from the menu bar. 2. The Save As dialog appears. 3. Modify the **filename** or **directory** to create a new copy. 4. Click **Save**. ### Method 3: Save All 1. Select **`File ŌåÆ Save All`** to save all open VIs in the workspace at once. 2. Each unsaved VI prompts a Save As dialog. ### Method 4: Save for Previous Version 1. Select **`File ŌåÆ Save for Previous Version`** to save the SubVI in an older LabVIEW format (for compatibility with colleagues using an older LabVIEW version). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Exam Note:** If you save a SubVI in a **newer LabVIEW version** and a calling VI was created in an **older version**, the calling VI may fail to load the SubVI. Always use **Save for Previous Version** when collaborating across LabVIEW versions. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Quick Comparison of Operations | Operation | Entry Point | Shortcut | Result | |---|---|---|---| | Open SubVI | Double-click node | ŌĆö | SubVI window opens | | Edit Icon | Double-click icon (FP) | ŌĆö | Icon Editor dialog | | Edit Connector | Right-click icon ŌåÆ Show Connector | ŌĆö | Connector pane visible | | Place SubVI | Functions Palette ŌåÆ Select a VI | ŌĆö | Node appears on BD | | Save SubVI | File menu | `Ctrl + S` | File saved to disk | | Save for Previous | File menu | ŌĆö | Backward-compatible file | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Anatomy of a SubVI ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[Front Panel Window] --> nodeB[Icon - Upper Right Corner] nodeA --> nodeC[Connector Pane - Terminals] nodeA --> nodeD[Controls - Inputs] nodeA --> nodeE[Indicators - Outputs] nodeF[Block Diagram Window] --> nodeG[Wired Logic Code] nodeF --> nodeH[SubVI Call Nodes] nodeB -. represents .-> nodeI[When placed on another BD] nodeC -. data flow interface .-> nodeI nodeG -. implemented in .-> nodeJ[SubVI file on disk] nodeD -. wired to .-> nodeC nodeE -. wired to .-> nodeC nodeC -. maps to .-> nodeG ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Workflow for Creating and Using a SubVI ```mermaid flowchart TD step1[Step 1: Build the mini-program
on Front Panel and Block Diagram] --> step2[Step 2: Define the Icon
double-click the icon in upper-right corner] step2 --> step3[Step 3: Define the Connector Pane
right-click icon then Show Connector] step3 --> step4[Step 4: Map Controls and Indicators
to Connector Terminals] step4 --> step5[Step 5: Save the file as a .vi
File then Save or Ctrl plus S] step5 --> step6[Step 6: Place on a calling VI
Select a VI from Functions Palette] step6 --> step7[Step 7: Wire Inputs and Outputs
on the calling VI block diagram] step7 --> step8[Step 8: Run and Test
Use Highlight Execution to debug] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Block Diagram Showing SubVI Placement ```mermaid flowchart LR sensor[Sensor Data
Numeric Control] --> wire1 wire1((Wire)) --> subNode[SubVI Node
Icon and Connector] threshold[Threshold Value
Numeric Control] --> wire2 wire2((Wire)) --> subNode subNode --> wire3 wire3((Wire)) --> alarm[Alarm Output
Boolean Indicator] subNode --> wire4 wire4((Wire)) --> display[Filtered Value
Numeric Indicator] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Opening and Editing Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart TD callVI[Calling VI block diagram] -->|Double-click| openSub[SubVI opens in new window] openSub --> editFP{Edit Front Panel
Add or modify controls} openSub --> editBD{Edit Block Diagram
Modify logic} openSub --> editIcon{Edit Icon
Use Icon Editor} openSub --> editConn{Edit Connector Pane
Map terminals} editFP --> save[Save with Ctrl plus S] editBD --> save editIcon --> save editConn --> save save --> callVI ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### ŌØō Question A1 ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]` **Define a SubVI in LabVIEW. List any four advantages of using SubVIs in Virtual Instrumentation programming.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **SubVI** is a stand-alone, modular VI that is incorporated into the block diagram of a higher-level (calling) VI. It is the graphical equivalent of a subroutine in textual programming. **Four advantages (1/2 mark each):** 1. **Modularity** ŌĆö Breaks a large, complex VI into smaller, manageable sub-units. 2. **Reusability** ŌĆö A SubVI built once can be called from many calling VIs, saving development time. 3. **Readability** ŌĆö Replacing large sections of block-diagram code with a single SubVI node makes the calling VI clean and self-documenting. 4. **Ease of Debugging** ŌĆö Each SubVI can be tested independently using Highlight Execution and probes. --- ### ŌØō Question A2 ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023]` **What is the purpose of the Connector Pane in a SubVI? Name the two parts of a SubVI that must be configured before it can be used on another block diagram.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Connector Pane** is the set of input and output terminals on a SubVI's Front Panel. It defines the **data-flow interface** between the calling VI and the SubVI ŌĆö specifying which data is passed IN to the SubVI (inputs) and which is returned OUT (outputs). **Two parts that must be configured (1 mark each):** 1. **Icon** ŌĆö A graphical picture that visually identifies the SubVI when placed on another block diagram. 2. **Connector Pane** ŌĆö A set of terminals mapped to the SubVI's controls and indicators to enable data transfer with the calling VI. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Internal Choice > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer an internal choice. Each question is split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. We provide TWO alternative question sets. --- ### ­¤ōś QUESTION A (14 Marks) ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper]` **Q. (a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure to create a SubVI in LabVIEW from an existing section of a block diagram. (7 Marks) **Q. (b)** Explain any three methods to place a SubVI on the block diagram of a calling VI. (7 Marks) #### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks **Procedure to create a SubVI from a selected block-diagram section:** 1. **Identify the section** of the block diagram in the calling VI that you want to convert into a SubVI. (1 mark) 2. **Select the section** by clicking and dragging the cursor (with the **Positioning tool** ŌĆö the arrow) over the wires, nodes, and terminals to be included. The selection will be highlighted with a moving dashed marquee. (1 mark) 3. From the **Edit menu**, select **`Create SubVI`**. (1 mark) 4. LabVIEW automatically: - Creates a new VI containing the selected block-diagram code. - Generates a **Connector Pane** with the appropriate number of input/output terminals. - Maps the wires entering the selection to **input controls** on the new SubVI's Front Panel. - Maps the wires leaving the selection to **output indicators** on the new SubVI's Front Panel. - Replaces the original selection in the calling VI with a **single SubVI node** bearing a default icon. (2 marks) 5. **Save the new SubVI** using `File ŌåÆ Save` (or `Ctrl + S`) and provide a meaningful filename. (1 mark) 6. **Optional refinement** ŌĆö Open the new SubVI, edit its **icon** (double-click the icon in the upper-right corner of the Front Panel) for better visual identification, and verify the **connector pane pattern**. (1 mark) #### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks **Three methods to place a SubVI on a calling VI's block diagram:** **Method 1: Drag-and-Drop from the File System (2 marks)** 1. Open Windows File Explorer. 2. Locate the `.vi` file of the SubVI. 3. Drag the file directly onto the target VI's Block Diagram window. 4. The SubVI node is dropped at the cursor's position; wire its terminals as needed. **Method 2: `Select a VI` from the Functions Palette (2 marks)** 1. Open the calling VI's Block Diagram. 2. Right-click an empty area to open the **Functions Palette**. 3. Click **`Select a VI...`** (located in the top-level palette area). 4. In the file dialog that appears, browse to the SubVI's `.vi` file and click **OK**. 5. The SubVI node appears attached to the cursor; click on the block diagram to place it. **Method 3: Drag from the Project Explorer (2 marks)** 1. Open the LabVIEW **Project Explorer** window. 2. Locate the SubVI in the project tree (under the appropriate target or folder). 3. Click and **drag** the SubVI from the project pane into the calling VI's Block Diagram. 4. Drop the node; wire inputs and outputs to the rest of the block diagram. (1 mark) --- ### ­¤ōŚ QUESTION B (14 Marks) ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023, Model Paper]` **Q. (a)** Explain the role of the **Icon** and the **Connector Pane** in a SubVI. How is the Connector Pane configured to map controls and indicators? (7 Marks) **Q. (b)** Describe the procedure to **open, edit, and save** a SubVI in LabVIEW. (7 Marks) #### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks **Role of the Icon (2 marks):** - The **icon** is a small graphical picture that visually represents the SubVI. - It appears in the **upper-right corner** of the SubVI's Front Panel and the **upper-left corner** of the Block Diagram. - When the SubVI is placed on another VI's block diagram, the **icon becomes the visual representation of the SubVI node**, helping the developer identify what the node does. - It is edited using the **Icon Editor** (`Right-click icon ŌåÆ Edit Icon`). **Role of the Connector Pane (2 marks):** - The **connector pane** is a set of small terminal squares on the SubVI's Front Panel. - It defines the **input and output ports** of the SubVI, enabling data to flow between the calling VI and the SubVI. - Each terminal corresponds to either a **Control** (input) or an **Indicator** (output). **Procedure to configure the Connector Pane (3 marks):** 1. Open the SubVI's Front Panel. 2. **Right-click the icon** in the upper-right corner and select **`Show Connector`**. The icon is replaced by a grid of terminal squares. 3. Click a **terminal square** on the connector pane ŌĆö it turns black, indicating it is selected. 4. Click a **Control** (for input) or **Indicator** (for output) on the Front Panel ŌĆö the terminal is now mapped (turns orange for numeric, green for Boolean, etc.). 5. Repeat for all desired inputs and outputs. 6. To change the **pattern** (number of terminals), right-click the connector pane ŌåÆ **Patterns** ŌåÆ select a layout (e.g., 2├Ś2, 4├Ś2├Ś2├Ś4). #### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks **Opening a SubVI (2 marks):** - On the calling VI's block diagram, **double-click** the SubVI node to open it in a new window. Alternatively, right-click the node and select **`Open`**. A third method is to double-click the SubVI file in the **Project Explorer**. **Editing a SubVI (3 marks):** - With the SubVI open, switch between Front Panel and Block Diagram using **`Ctrl + E`**. - Modify the logic on the Block Diagram by adding/removing functions, structures, or wires. - Edit the **Icon** by double-clicking the icon in the upper-right corner of the Front Panel. - Edit the **Connector Pane** by right-clicking the icon ŌåÆ `Show Connector`, then remap terminals to controls/indicators. - Use **Highlight Execution** (the lightbulb icon) to debug the SubVI's logic. **Saving a SubVI (2 marks):** - Use **`Ctrl + S`** to save.
