Ōöé  Ōöé  ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ  Ōöé    Ōöé
Ōöé  Ōöé  Ōöé  Case: Timeout [-1, 0, or N]            Ōöé  Ōöé    Ōöé
Ōöé  Ōöé  ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś  Ōöé    Ōöé
Ōöé  ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś    Ōöé
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
       Ō¢▓                                            Ōöé
       Ōöé                                            Ō¢╝
   [Outer While Loop: Stop Button]
```

**Terminals (3 marks):**
- **Timeout Terminal** ŌĆö I32 milliseconds.
- **Event Data Node** ŌĆö Variant (decomposable into source, type, time, OldVal/NewVal).
- **Event Filter Node** ŌĆö Boolean (only in Filter-event cases).

**Architecture explanation (4 marks):** The Event Structure is a stack-based, multi-case structure that must be placed inside a loop. It blocks on registered events and executes the matching case. Each event has an event source (control/VI/application) and an event type (notify/filter). The structure cannot be configured to handle the same event twice in the same case (no duplicates); use multiple structures if needed.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Polling CPU Load (3 marks):**

$$
L_{poll} = \frac{T_{active}}{T_{period}} \times 100\% = \frac{2\,\text{ms}}{30\,\text{ms}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
L_{poll} = 0.0667 \times 100\% = \boxed{6.67\%}
$$

**Event-Driven CPU Load (2 marks):**

$$
L_{evt} = \frac{0\,\text{ms}}{T_{total}} \times 100\% = \boxed{\approx 0\%}
$$

**Justification (2 marks):** In polling, the loop actively queries the control value every 30 ms, regardless of whether a change occurred. The CPU remains busy for 6.67% of the time. In the Event-driven architecture, the structure sleeps on the event queue with `timeout = -1`; the OS only wakes the structure when a genuine event occurs, releasing the CPU otherwise. This yields a **6.67% ŌåÆ ~0%** drop in CPU consumption, making the system scalable, deterministic, and energy-efficient.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain **Static** and **Dynamic** events. Compare them in a table. Describe a use case where Dynamic events are mandatory. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a step-by-step design, explain how to implement a **User Event** in LabVIEW to send a numeric value from a sub-VI to the main VI whenever the value exceeds a threshold. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Static Events (2 marks):** Events whose event source is a **front-panel control that exists at edit time**. Registered automatically by the Event Structure; no explicit registration code required. Easy to use but inflexible at runtime.

**Dynamic Events (2 marks):** Events registered **programmatically at runtime** using the `Register For Events` node and `User Event` nodes. Sources can be created in code (e.g., for controls spawned at runtime).

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Property | Static Event | Dynamic Event |
|---|---|---|
| Registration | Automatic | Manual (`Register For Events`) |
| Source visibility at edit time | Required | Not required |
| Runtime flexibility | Low | High |
| Can fire from sub-VI | Difficult | Native via User Event refnum |
| Use case | Fixed UI with known controls | Plugin-based or runtime-generated UI |

**Mandatory Use Case (1 mark):** A **plugin-style data acquisition panel** where each plugin sub-VI must report its own user-defined threshold-exceedance event back to the main shell ŌĆö only Dynamic events can do this cleanly.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step-by-Step Design (7 marks):**

1. **In the Sub-VI (3 marks):**
   - Place a `User Event` node (Functions ŌåÆ Dialog & User Interface) with **DBL** data type to create a *User Event Refnum*.
   - Wire this refnum to a **connector pane terminal** of the sub-VI.
   - Inside the sub-VI's loop, compare the numeric output to the threshold:
     - If exceeded, call `Generate User Event` and pass the value.
     - If not, skip.

2. **In the Main VI (4 marks):**
   - Wire the sub-VI's User Event Refnum output to a `Register For Events` node.
   - The output of `Register For Events` (a Dynamic Events refnum) feeds into the **Event Source** of the Event Structure.
   - Configure the Event Structure's event to listen to the **User Event** type.
   - Inside the case, use the **Event Data Node** to extract the numeric value and act on it (display, log, alarm).
   - Outside the loop, call `Unregister For Events` to release resources.

```
Sub-VI Loop:
  Numeric (DBL) ŌåÆ > Threshold ? ŌåÆ Yes ŌåÆ Generate User Event(value)
                                       ŌåÆ No  ŌåÆ Idle
                                         Ōåō
                              User Event Out (Refnum) ŌåÆ Main VI

Main VI:
  Register For Events (refnum) ŌåÆ Event Source
                                  Ōåō
                          Event Structure
                                  Ōåō
                          Display / Log value
```

---

## ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Placing an Event Structure OUTSIDE a loop (0/14):** The structure will execute **exactly once** and then stop. It MUST be inside a `While Loop` (or `For Loop` with auto-indexing).
> 2. **Forgetting the `Panel Close?` Filter event (lose 1ŌĆō2 marks):** The default "Stop" button on a VI's title bar **does not** automatically stop a While Loop ŌĆö the loop's stop terminal is the only stop source unless a `Panel Close?` event is wired.
> 3. **Polling a control inside an Event case (logic error):** Use the **Event Data Node** (`OldVal`, `NewVal`) instead of reading the control's terminal ŌĆö the terminal value may not be updated yet.
> 4. **Setting `timeout = 0` in a tight loop (causes 100% CPU):** Use `timeout = -1` for idle waits, never `0`, in a high-priority production VI.
> 5. **Using `Value (Signaling)` property inside an Event case ŌåÆ deadlock:** The Value property's locking mechanism conflicts with the Event Structure's own lock. Use `Value` (non-signaling) inside event cases.
> 6. **Missing the Timeout case entirely (silent failure):** If you set a non-(-1) timeout, you **must** provide a Timeout case; otherwise LabVIEW throws an error at runtime.
> 7. **Confusing `Event Data Node` with regular terminals:** The Event Data Node is on the **inside-left** of the case; it is not a regular data tunnel.

---

## ­¤öü Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Ō£ģ **Event Structure** is a **stack-based, multi-case** structure that must be placed **inside a loop**.
- Ō£ģ It enables **event-driven programming** ŌĆö CPU drops to **~0%** when idle (vs. ~3ŌĆō10% for polling).
- Ō£ģ **Three terminals:** `Timeout (I32)`, `Event Data Node (Variant)`, `Event Filter Node (Boolean, Filter-only)`.
- Ō£ģ **Timeout = -1** means wait forever; **0** means no wait; **N** means wait N ms.
- Ō£ģ **Notify events** react *after* an action; **Filter events** can *abort* the action *before* it propagates.
- Ō£ģ **Static events** are auto-registered; **Dynamic events** require `Register For Events` and are mandatory for runtime-generated UIs and cross-VI event communication.
- Ō£ģ **User Events** are the standard way to fire custom events from sub-VIs to main VIs (carry user-defined data types).
- Ō£ģ Always handle the **Panel Close?** event explicitly; the title-bar X does not stop a While Loop by itself.
- Ō£ģ Use **Event Data Node** (not the control terminal) to read event payload inside the case.
- Ō£ģ Never use `Value (Signaling)` property inside an Event case ŌĆö it causes a **deadlock**.
- Ō£ģ For dynamic events, always call `Unregister For Events` after the loop to prevent memory leaks.
- Ō£ģ High-yield formulas to memorize:
  - $L_{poll} = \dfrac{T_{active}}{T_{period}} \times 100\%$
  - $L_{evt} \approx 0\%$ (when idle)
  - $Q_{max} = 1$ (Value Change discards older events)
- Ō£ģ **Examiner-favorite topics:** CPU-load comparison derivations, Static-vs-Dynamic event tables, Filter-event use cases, dynamic User Event design from sub-VIs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LabVIEW MathScript
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT745 - VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** LabVIEW MathScript ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **LabVIEW MathScript** is a text-based, mathematical scripting environment embedded within the National Instruments (NI) LabVIEW development platform. It provides a syntax highly compatible with **MATLAB** (MathWorks) and supports a rich library of mathematical, signal-processing, and analytical functions executed in an interpreted, interactive manner. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEICT745 ŌĆō Module 3)**, MathScript is defined as: > *"LabVIEW MathScript is an add-on textual programming environment that allows engineers and scientists to write mathematical algorithms in a syntax similar to MATLAB, enabling seamless integration of script-based math with graphical dataflow programming."* Two primary components deliver this functionality: - **MathScript Window (Interactive Mode):** A standalone interactive command-line environment for prototyping, debugging, and command execution. - **MathScript Node (Embedded Mode):** A block-diagram node that allows users to drop `.m`-style scripts directly inside a LabVIEW Virtual Instrument (VI), with terminal-based data input/output. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The "Two-Tool Workshop":** > Imagine an electronics workshop. You have a **power drill (LabVIEW Graphical Programming)** for fast prototyping and a **precision screwdriver set (MathScript Textual Math)** for fine, intricate mathematical work. MathScript is the precision screwdriver that performs exact mathematical calculations while the drill (graphical VIs) handles data acquisition, instrumentation, and hardware control. Together, they make a complete toolset ŌĆö this is the **Hybrid Programming Paradigm** of LabVIEW. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight for KTU Board Exams:** > Whenever a question asks "How does MathScript integrate with LabVIEW?", the answer must always mention the **MathScript Node** as the bridge between the textual and graphical worlds. ### 1.3 Why MathScript Exists ŌĆö The Problem It Solves Many engineering computations (matrix algebra, FFTs, IIR/FIR filter design, curve fitting, statistical modeling) are far more compact to write as **textual code** rather than wiring dozens of graphical primitives. MathScript addresses this by: 1. **Rapid Prototyping:** Type commands at the prompt ŌåÆ get instant results. 2. **Portability:** Code written in MathScript can often be migrated to/from MATLAB with minimal changes. 3. **Hybrid Workflow:** Use text for math, graphics for I/O ŌĆö best of both worlds. 4. **Reusability:** Save scripts as `.m` files and load them across multiple VIs. ### 1.4 Core Capabilities Summary - **Mathematical:** Arithmetic, linear algebra (matrix inverse, eigenvalues), trigonometry, calculus. - **Signal Processing:** FFT, IFFT, convolution, digital filter design. - **Statistical:** Mean, median, standard deviation, histogram, curve fitting. - **I/O:** Load/save `.csv`, `.txt`, `.tdms`, `.lvm` files. - **Custom Functions:** User-defined functions in `.m` files callable from the MathScript Window. > [!IMPORTANT] > MathScript is **interpreted**, not compiled. This makes debugging interactive but introduces overhead compared to native LabVIEW primitives. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The MathScript Architecture MathScript consists of **two distinct environments** operating on the same scripting engine: 1. **MathScript Interactive Window** - Launched via: *Tools ŌåÆ MathScript Window*. - Features a **Command Window**, **Variable Viewer**, and **Output Window**. - Supports live execution, plotting (`plot`, `stem`, `subplot`). - Used for quick calculations, debugging, and algorithm prototyping. 2. **MathScript Node** - Inserted onto the Block Diagram via *Functions Palette ŌåÆ Mathematics ŌåÆ Script & Formula ŌåÆ MathScript Node*. - Accepts **input terminals** on the left and **output terminals** on the right. - Variables declared inside the node become output terminals automatically. - Executes the script each time the surrounding VI runs. ### 2.2 Data Types in MathScript MathScript natively handles the following scalar and array types: | Type | Description | Example | |------|-------------|---------| | `double` | 64-bit floating-point | `x = 3.14159` | | `int32` | 32-bit signed integer | `n = int32(10)` | | `complex` | Real + imaginary pair | `z = 3 + 4i` | | `string` | Character array | `s = 'KTU'` | | `vector` | 1-D array | `v = [1, 2, 3]` | | `matrix` | 2-D array | `M = [1 2; 3 4]` | | `boolean` | TRUE / FALSE | `flag = true` | | `struct` | Named field container | `s.field1 = 10` | ### 2.3 Operator Categories - **Arithmetic:** `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `^`, `.*` (element-wise multiply), `./` (element-wise divide). - **Relational:** `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=`, `==`, `~=`. - **Logical:** `&&`, `||`, `~`. - **Matrix:** `'` (transpose), `*` (matrix multiply), `\ ` (left division). - **Colon operator:** `start:step:stop` for vector generation. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains **high-yield formulas** that frequently appear in KTU university examinations for MathScript and signal-processing subtopics. | Category | Formula / Syntax | Description | |----------|------------------|-------------| | Vector generation | `x = a:step:b` | Creates vector from $a$ to $b$ in steps of $step$ | | Matrix indexing | `A(i, j)` | Access element in row $i$, column $j$ | | FFT | `Y = fft(x)` | Computes Discrete Fourier Transform of vector $x$ | | IFFT | `y = ifft(Y)` | Inverse DFT, $y[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} Y[k] e^{j2\pi kn/N}$ | | Convolution | `c = conv(a, b)` | Linear convolution of two sequences | | Mean | `m = mean(x)` | Average $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | | Standard deviation | `s = std(x)` | $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | | Filter design | `[b, a] = butter(n, Wn)` | Butterworth IIR coefficients | | FIR design | `b = fir1(n, Wn)` | Window-based FIR filter, $W_n = f_c / (f_s/2)$ | | Element-wise | `C = A .* B` | $c_{ij} = a_{ij} \cdot b_{ij}$ | | Matrix inverse | `B = inv(A)` | $B = A^{-1}$ where $A \cdot B = I$ | | Eigenvalues | `V = eig(A)` | Roots of $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$ | | Polynomial fit | `p = polyfit(x, y, n)` | Fits $n$-th order polynomial to $(x,y)$ | | Polynomial eval | `y = polyval(p, x)` | Evaluates polynomial $p$ at $x$ | | Load data | `d = load('file.csv')` | Reads numeric file into variable $d$ | ### 2.5 Programming Constructs Supported MathScript supports standard structured-programming flow: - **For Loop:** `for i = 1:10, y(i) = i^2; end` - **While Loop:** `while err > 1e-6, x = x + 1; end` - **IfŌĆōElse:** `if (x > 0), y = 1; else, y = 0; end` - **Switch (multi-branch):** `switch value, case 1, ... otherwise, ... end` - **Functions:** Define in `.m` files with `function y = myfunc(x)`. ### 2.6 MathScript vs MATLAB vs Pure LabVIEW | Feature | MathScript | MATLAB | LabVIEW (G) | |---------|-----------|--------|-------------| | License cost | Bundled with LV | Separate | Bundled with LV | | Hardware I/O | Via MathScript Node | Via toolboxes | Native, extensive | | Syntax style | Textual, MATLAB-like | Textual | Graphical / dataflow | | Compilation | Interpreted | JIT-compiled | Compiled | | Plotting | Basic (`plot`, `stem`) | Advanced | Advanced, interactive | | Best for | Math + LabVIEW hybrid | Standalone math | Real-time DAQ systems | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** In industrial test stands (e.g., vibration analysis of rotating machinery), MathScript is used to compute RMS, FFT, and THD on data acquired by LabVIEW DAQmx. This hybrid pattern is the **industry standard** for NI-based test systems. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Discrete Fourier Transform via MathScript **Problem Statement:** Compute the magnitude spectrum of a signal composed of two sinusoids: $$x(t) = \sin(2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot t) + 0.5 \cdot \sin(2\pi \cdot 120 \cdot t)$$ sampled at $f_s = 1000$ Hz for duration $T = 1$ second. **Step 1 ŌĆö Parameter Setup** $$ f_s = 1000 \;\text{Hz}, \quad T = 1\;\text{s}, \quad N = f_s \cdot T = 1000 $$ In MathScript: ``` fs = 1000; T = 1; N = fs * T; t = (0:N-1) / fs; % time vector ``` **Step 2 ŌĆö Synthesize the Signal** $$ x(t) = \sin(2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot t) + 0.5 \cdot \sin(2\pi \cdot 120 \cdot t) $$ MathScript code: ``` x = sin(2*pi*50*t) + 0.5*sin(2*pi*120*t); ``` **Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the FFT** The Discrete Fourier Transform is: $$ X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot e^{-j 2\pi k n / N} $$ MathScript code: ``` Y = fft(x); ``` **Step 4 ŌĆö Compute Magnitude Spectrum** $$ \vert X[k] \vert = \sqrt{\Re\{X[k]\}^2 + \Im\{X[k]\}^2} $$ The two-sided spectrum needs single-sided conversion: $$ X_{\text{single}}[k] = \frac{2}{N} \cdot \vert X[k] \vert, \quad 0 \le k \le N/2 $$ MathScript code: ``` mag = (2/N) * abs(Y(1:N/2 + 1)); f = (0:N/2) * (fs / N); ``` **Step 5 ŌĆö Display Result** ``` plot(f, mag); xlabel('Frequency (Hz)'); ylabel('Magnitude'); title('Magnitude Spectrum via MathScript FFT'); ``` **Analytical Result:** $$ f_{\text{peak1}} = 50\;\text{Hz},\;\; \vert X \vert = 1.0 \qquad ; \qquad f_{\text{peak2}} = 120\;\text{Hz},\;\; \vert X \vert = 0.5 $$ This matches the input amplitudes (50 Hz at full amplitude, 120 Hz at half amplitude), confirming the MathScript pipeline operates correctly. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö User-Defined MathScript Function **Goal:** Create a reusable function to compute the **Root Mean Square (RMS)** of any input vector. **Mathematical Definition of RMS:** $$ x_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2} $$ **Step-by-Step MathScript Derivation:** **Step A** ŌĆö Define the function in a file named `rmscalc.m`: ``` function r = rmscalc(x) N = length(x); sq_sum = 0; for i = 1:N sq_sum = sq_sum + x(i)^2; end r = sqrt(sq_sum / N); end ``` **Step B** ŌĆö Vectorize the loop for efficiency: Since $\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2$ equals the dot product $x \cdot x$, we can write: ``` r = sqrt((x * x') / length(x)); ``` **Step C** ŌĆö Or use the built-in (cleaner): ``` r = sqrt(mean(x .* x)); ``` **Step D** ŌĆö Verification with sinusoidal input $x(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$: For a sinusoid, the analytical RMS is: $$ x_{\text{rms}} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}} $$ **Numerical check:** For $A = 10$, expected $x_{\text{rms}} = 7.0711$. MathScript verification code: ``` A = 10; t = 0:0.0001:1; x = A * sin(2*pi*5*t); r = sqrt(mean(x .* x)); % Expected: ~7.0711 ``` ``` % Result: % r = 7.0711 ``` **Result is correct** with relative error $< 10^{-4}$. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 ŌĆö Matrix Operations and Polynomial Curve Fit **Problem:** Fit a 2nd-degree polynomial $p(t) = a_2 t^2 + a_1 t + a_0$ to noisy measurements. **Mathematical Derivation:** Given data points $(t_i, y_i)$, the polynomial fit minimizes the squared error: $$ E = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i - p(t_i) \right]^2 $$ In matrix form: $$ \mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{c} = \mathbf{y}, \quad \text{where } A_{ij} = t_i^{j-1} $$ The least-squares solution is: $$ \mathbf{c} = (\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A})^{-1} \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{y} $$ **MathScript implementation:** ``` t = 0:0.1:10; y = 2*t.^2 - 3*t + 5 + 0.5*randn(size(t)); % noisy quadratic p = polyfit(t, y, 2); % returns [a2, a1, a0] y_fit = polyval(p, t); plot(t, y, 'o', t, y_fit, '-'); legend('Noisy data', '2nd-order fit'); ``` **Expected fitted coefficients:** $$ a_2 \approx 2.0, \quad a_1 \approx -3.0, \quad a_0 \approx 5.0 $$ The slight deviation is due to the Gaussian noise term $0.5 \cdot \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ added in the synthetic data. ## 4. Discuss the role of input/output terminals. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a MathScript program (as if inside a MathScript Node) to: - Generate a 2 Hz sine wave sampled at 100 Hz for 2 seconds. - Add Gaussian noise of standard deviation 0.2. - Compute and display the RMS value of the noisy signal. - Apply a 3rd-order Butterworth low-pass filter with cutoff 5 Hz. - Compute and display the RMS of the filtered signal. Explain the result mathematically. **(7 marks)** --- ##### Model Solution **(a) MathScript Architecture (7 marks):** **MathScript Engine ŌĆö Central Interpreter (2 marks):** The MathScript Engine is the core interpreter that parses and executes `.m`-compatible scripts. It hosts mathematical libraries (linear algebra, FFT, statistics), file I/O, and plotting primitives. Both the MathScript Window and the MathScript Node delegate execution to this single engine. **MathScript Window ŌĆö Interactive Mode (2 marks):** - Launched via *Tools ŌåÆ MathScript Window*. - Comprises a Command Window, Variable Viewer, and Output Window. - Used for prototyping algorithms, debugging, and command-line exploration. - Variables persist in the workspace until cleared with `clear`. **MathScript Node ŌĆö Embedded Mode (2 marks):** - Dropped onto the LabVIEW Block Diagram from *Functions ŌåÆ Mathematics ŌåÆ Script & Formula ŌåÆ MathScript Node*. - **Input terminals** (left border) feed data into the script from graphical wires. - **Output terminals** (right border) are auto-created when a variable is assigned in the script. - Allows hybrid VI design: textual math + graphical I/O and control. **Role of Terminals (1 mark):** Terminals make the script **dataflow-aware**, meaning LabVIEW executes the node each time its inputs change, and outputs update automatically upon script completion. **Block Diagram:** ``` ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ Ōöé MathScript Window ŌöéŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ Ōöé (Interactive Mode) Ōöé Ōöé ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś Ōöé Ō¢╝ ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ Ōöé MathScript ŌöéŌŚĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ User-defined Ōöé Engine Ōöé .m files ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś Ō¢▓ ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ Ōöé Ōöé MathScript Node ŌöéŌöĆŌöĆŌöś Ōöé (Inside LabVIEW VI) Ōöé Ōöé ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ Ōöé Ōöé ŌöéInputsŌöéŌåÆ Ōöé Script ŌöéŌåÆŌöé Outputs Ōöé Ōöé ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś Ōöé ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś ``` --- **(b) MathScript Program with Filter Analysis (7 marks):** **Step 1 ŌĆö Parameter Setup (1 mark):** ``` fs = 100; % Sampling frequency T = 2; % Duration in seconds t = 0:1/fs:T-1/fs; % Time vector of length N=200 N = length(t); ``` **Step 2 ŌĆö Generate Noisy Sine Wave (1 mark):** $$ x_{\text{noisy}}(t) = \sin(2\pi \cdot 2 \cdot t) + n(t), \quad n \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\; 0.2^2) $$ ``` x_clean = sin(2*pi*2*t); noise = 0.2 * randn(1, N); x_noisy = x_clean + noise; ``` **Step 3 ŌĆö RMS of Noisy Signal (1.5 marks):** The RMS formula is: $$ x_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2} $$ ``` rms_noisy = sqrt(mean(x_noisy .* x_noisy)); ``` Expected analytical RMS of clean 2 Hz sine: $$ x_{\text{rms,clean}} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071 $$ The noisy RMS will be slightly higher due to the added noise variance $\sigma^2 = 0.04$: $$ x_{\text{rms,noisy}} \approx \sqrt{0.5 + 0.04} = \sqrt{0.54} \approx 0.7348 $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Design Butterworth Filter (1.5 marks):** Normalized cutoff frequency: $$ W_n = \frac{f_c}{f_s/2} = \frac{5}{50} = 0.1 $$ ``` [b, a] = butter(3, 0.1); % 3rd-order Butterworth, Wn = 0.1 ``` **Step 5 ŌĆö Apply Filter and Compute New RMS (1 mark):** ``` y_filtered = filter(b, a, x_noisy); rms_filtered = sqrt(mean(y_filtered .* y_filtered)); ``` **Step 6 ŌĆö Result Summary (1 mark):** | Quantity | Expected Value | Comment | |----------|---------------|---------| | `rms_noisy` | ~0.7348 | Higher due to noise | | `rms_filtered` | ~0.7071 | Approaches clean signal RMS | **Interpretation:** The Butterworth low-pass filter attenuates the high-frequency noise components while preserving the 2 Hz tone, causing the RMS to converge back toward the analytical clean-signal value of $1/\sqrt{2}$. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks ŌĆō Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023, Adapted]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain how LabVIEW MathScript integrates with the LabVIEW development environment. Compare MathScript with MATLAB in terms of syntax compatibility, hardware I/O, and licensing. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design a MathScript program to: - Acquire (or simulate) a 1-second signal sampled at 1000 Hz containing 50 Hz and 200 Hz components. - Compute the magnitude spectrum using FFT. - Identify the two dominant frequencies. - Apply a band-stop filter centered at 200 Hz to suppress the 200 Hz tone. - Recompute the magnitude spectrum and verify that only the 50 Hz component remains prominent. Provide the complete MathScript code with explanations. **(7 marks)** --- ##### Model Solution **(a) MathScript Integration and Comparison (7 marks):** **Integration with LabVIEW (3 marks):** LabVIEW MathScript integrates into LabVIEW through two distinct paths: 1. **Interactive Path:** The MathScript Window operates as a standalone IDE where users write, run, and debug scripts. Scripts saved as `.m` files can be reloaded in subsequent sessions. 2. **Embedded Path:** The MathScript Node is placed on the Block Diagram. Ōöé Ōöé Ōöé Ōöé b = fft(a); Ōöé Ōöé Ōöé Ōöé c = abs(b); Ōöé Ōöé ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ Ōöé Ōöé output1 = c Ōöé Ōöé Ōöé Waveform ŌöéŌŚĆŌöĆŌöĆwireŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöé ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś Ōöé Ōöé Graph Ōöé ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś ``` **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Criterion | MathScript | MATLAB | |-----------|-----------|--------| | **Syntax compatibility** | Subset of MATLAB syntax; supports `for`, `while`, `if`, matrix ops, FFT, filter design | Full MATLAB language | | **Hardware I/O** | Native via LabVIEW DAQmx, VISA, FPGA | Requires separate toolboxes (Data Acquisition, Instrument Control) | | **Licensing** | Bundled with LabVIEW Full/Pro license | Separate MathWorks license; expensive | | **Plotting** | Basic (`plot`, `stem`, `subplot`) | Extensive (handle graphics, GUI builder) | | **Deployment** | Compiles into LabVIEW executables (.exe) | Requires MATLAB Runtime or license | | **Speed** | Interpreted; moderate | JIT-optimized; faster for large math | --- **(b) MathScript Program ŌĆö Spectral Analysis and Notch Filtering (7 marks):** **Step 1 ŌĆö Signal Generation (1 mark):** $$ x(t) = \sin(2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot t) + \sin(2\pi \cdot 200 \cdot t) $$ ``` fs = 1000; T = 1; t = 0:1/fs:T-1/fs; N = length(t); % N = 1000 x = sin(2*pi*50*t) + sin(2*pi*200*t); ``` **Step 2 ŌĆö FFT and Magnitude Spectrum (1.5 marks):** ``` Y = fft(x); mag = (2/N) * abs(Y(1:N/2+1)); f_axis = (0:N/2) * (fs/N); ``` The DFT is: $$ X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot e^{-j2\pi kn/N} $$ Peaks should appear at $f = 50$ Hz and $f = 200$ Hz. **Step 3 ŌĆö Identify Dominant Frequencies (1 mark):** ``` [peaks, locs] = findpeaks(mag); dominant_freqs = f_axis(locs); % Expected: [50, 200] ``` **Step 4 ŌĆö Design Band-Stop (Notch) Filter at 200 Hz (2 marks):** Normalized notch frequencies: $$ W_{\text{low}} = \frac{195}{500} = 0.39, \quad W_{\text{high}} = \frac{205}{500} = 0.41 $$ ``` [b, a] = butter(3, [0.39, 0.41], 'stop'); y = filter(b, a, x); ``` The filter transfer function: $$ H(z) = \frac{B(z)}{A(z)} = \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + b_M z^{-M}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + a_N z^{-N}} $$ Attenuates the band $[195, 205]$ Hz by ~18 dB for a 3rd-order Butterworth stop-band. **Step 5 ŌĆö Recompute Magnitude Spectrum (1 mark):** ``` Y2 = fft(y); mag2 = (2/N) * abs(Y2(1:N/2+1)); plot(f_axis, mag, f_axis, mag2); legend('Original', 'After Notch Filter'); xlabel('Frequency (Hz)'); ylabel('Magnitude'); ``` **Step 6 ŌĆö Verification (0.5 marks):** | Frequency | Original Magnitude | After Filter Magnitude | |-----------|--------------------|-----------------------| | 50 Hz | 1.0 | ~1.0 (preserved) | | 200 Hz | 1.0 | < 0.13 (attenuated by ~18 dB) | **Conclusion:** The 200 Hz tone is successfully suppressed while the 50 Hz component passes through with negligible attenuation. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Students Make ŌĆö Avoid These to Secure Full Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing MathScript with MATLAB (1ŌĆō2 mark loss):** MathScript supports a **subset** of MATLAB syntax. Statements like `parfor`, advanced OOP, App Designer, and Simulink are **not** available in MathScript. Writing "MathScript is identical to MATLAB" will lose marks. > > 2. **Forgetting Input/Output Terminal Behavior:** In the MathScript Node, variables defined inside the script **only** appear as output terminals if they are assigned (e.g., `y = ...`). Declared but unassigned variables stay local. > > 3. **Ignoring Normalization:** Filter cutoff frequencies must be **normalized to Nyquist**: $W_n = f_c / (f_s/2)$. Writing `butter(3, 5)` with $f_s = 100$ is **wrong** ŌĆö the correct form is `butter(3, 0.1)`. > > 4. **Wrong FFT Scaling:** The raw FFT output magnitude scales with $N$. For physical amplitude recovery, multiply by $\frac{2}{N}$ (one-sided spectrum). > > 5. **Mixing Single- and Double-Precision:** MathScript defaults to `double`. Implicit type casting can introduce subtle bugs when interfacing with LabVIEW I32 or I16 controls. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö LabVIEW MathScript:** > > Ō£ģ MathScript is a **textual math environment** with **MATLAB-like syntax**, embedded inside LabVIEW. > > Ō£ģ **Two access modes:** MathScript Window (interactive) and MathScript Node (embedded in VI). > > Ō£ģ **MathScript Node** is the integration bridge ŌĆö it has input terminals (left) and output terminals (right). > > Ō£ģ Data types: scalar, vector, matrix, complex, string, struct, boolean. > > Ō£ģ Element-wise ops use dot-prefix: `.*`, `./`, `.^`. > > Ō£ģ **FFT:** `Y = fft(x)`; for one-sided amplitude, scale by `2/N`. > > Ō£ģ **Filter design:** `butter`, `cheby1`, `fir1` ŌĆö always use **normalized cutoff** $W_n = f_c / (f_s/2)$. > > Ō£ģ Programming constructs: `for`, `while`, `ifŌĆōelse`, `switch`, `function`. > > Ō£ģ User-defined functions stored in `.m` files are reusable across the MathScript Window and Node. > > Ō£ģ MathScript supports **file I/O** for `.csv`, `.txt`, `.tdms`, `.lvm`. > > Ō£ģ MathScript is **interpreted**, not compiled ŌĆö execution speed is slower than native compiled G-code. > > Ō£ģ For real-time or FPGA targets, **avoid MathScript** and use native LabVIEW primitives instead. > > Ō£ģ Industry use case: hybrid systems where LabVIEW handles DAQ/HMI and MathScript performs advanced signal-processing math.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT745 - VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Strings and File I/O: Creating String Controls and Indicators, String Functions, Editing, Formatting and Parsing Strings, Formatting Strings, Configuring String Controls and Indicators, Basics of File Input/Output, Choosing a File I/O Format, LabVIEW Data Directory, File I/O VIs, Creating a Relative Path

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ­¤ōś MODULE 3 ŌĆö STRINGS AND FILE I/O IN LABVIEW

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 String Controls and Indicators ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> A **String** in LabVIEW is a sequence of displayable or non-displayable **ASCII (American Standard Code for Information Interchange)** characters. Strings provide an independent, platform-independent format for information and are the primary medium for storing numeric data to disk, displaying text on the front panel, and communicating with instruments via **VISA (Virtual Instrument Software Architecture)**.

A **String Control** is a front-panel object used to **input** text (e.g., a file path typed by the user, a GPIB command), while a **String Indicator** is a front-panel object used to **display or output** text (e.g., a measured temperature, an error message, instrument response).

**Physical/Encoding Standard Metric:**
- LabVIEW strings are stored as **unsigned 8-bit integers (U8)**.
- Default display mode: **Normal Display** (shows printable ASCII characters only).
- A string can also be inspected in **Hex Display** (raw byte values), **Password Display** (masked dots), or **ŌĆś\ŌĆÖ Codes Display** (backslash escape codes for non-printable characters).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "Strings are the Universal Language Glue" ­¤¦®

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy**
> Think of a LabVIEW string as the **envelope of a postal letter**:
> - The **front-panel string control** = the **blank envelope** you write on.
> - The **string indicator** = the **envelope you read** when it comes back.
> - The **block diagram wire (pink line)** = the **postal service** that carries the message between sender and receiver.
> - **String Functions** (Concatenate, Subset, Match Pattern) = the **post-office tools** that let you stamp, cut, fold, or translate the message.
> - **File I/O VIs** = the **filing cabinet clerk** who takes your envelope and either stores it (Write) or retrieves it (Read).

Just as postal envelopes must follow a standard format (address, stamp, postcode) for delivery to work, **LabVIEW strings must follow consistent formatting rules** so that your VIs can reliably parse, transmit, and store data ŌĆö and the **File I/O subsystem** is the librarian who decides whether the envelope is filed in a `.txt` ledger, an Excel spreadsheet, or a compressed `.zip` archive.

---

### 1.3 File Input/Output (File I/O) ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **File I/O** refers to the transfer of data between **memory (RAM)** and **files on disk**. In LabVIEW, File I/O operations are performed using the **File I/O VIs and Functions** palette (`Programming ŌåÆ File I/O`), enabling the VIs to read pre-recorded data, save measurement results, and log real-time waveforms.

**Three Universal Steps in Every File I/O Operation:**
1. **Open** ŌĆö Create or open the file (`Open/Create/Replace File.vi`).
2. **Read or Write** ŌĆö Perform the data transfer (`Write to Text File.vi`, `Read from Text File.vi`, etc.).
3. **Close** ŌĆö Release the file handle and free resources (`Close File.vi`).

> [!NOTE]
> **The File Handle (Refnum)**
> A *refnum* is a unique identifier ŌĆö like a library card number ŌĆö that LabVIEW assigns to an open file. The refnum is passed between the Open, Read/Write, and Close VIs to track which file is being operated on. Failing to close a refnum results in **data loss** (data sitting in the write buffer is never flushed to disk) and a **memory leak**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 Creating String Controls and Indicators

### Step-by-Step Logic
- Right-click the **Front Panel** ŌåÆ **Controls Palette ŌåÆ Modern ŌåÆ String & Path** (or `Classic ŌåÆ String`).
- Drag and drop **String Control** (input) or **String Indicator** (output) onto the front panel.
- The corresponding **terminal** (pink wire icon `abc`) appears on the **Block Diagram**.
- Use the **Operating tool** (finger cursor) to type into a control; use the **Positioning tool** (arrow) to resize, move, or label.

### Display Modes of a String (Configurable)

| Display Mode | Behavior | KTU Exam Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Normal Display** | Shows printable ASCII characters; non-printable appear as boxes. | Default. Use for user inputs. |
| **ŌĆś\\ŌĆÖ Codes Display** | Shows backslash escape sequences for special chars (`\r`, `\n`, `\t`, `\00`). | Use when designing communication protocols. |
| **Password Display** | Replaces every character with a bullet/asterisk. | Use for security fields. |
| **Hex Display** | Shows raw U8 byte values (e.g., `48 65 6C 6C 6F` = "Hello"). | Use for binary data inspection & debugging. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** Students are often asked to **state the four display modes** of a string and when to use each. Memorize the table above.

---

## 2.2 String Functions (Programming ŌåÆ String Palette)

| Function | Inputs | Output | Real-Time Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **String Length** | `string` | `length` (I32) | Validate user input length. |
| **Concatenate Strings** | `string1`, `string2`, ŌĆ” | `concatenated string` | Build file paths or messages. |
| **Subset String** | `string`, `offset`, `length` | `subset` | Extract a specific character range. |
| **Match Pattern** | `string`, `regular expression` | `offset`, `matched substring`, `rest of string` | Parse delimited data (CSV). |
| **String Subset** | `string`, `start`, `length` | `subset` | Cut a piece from the middle. |
| **Replace Substring** | `string`, `offset`, `length`, `substring` | `result string` | Edit a string in place. |
| **Search and Replace** | `string`, `search string`, `replace string` | `result string` | Global find/replace. |
| **Format Into String** | `format string`, inputs | `result string` | Convert numbers ŌåÆ ASCII for logging. |
| **Scan From String** | `input string`, `format string` | outputs (numeric) | Convert ASCII ŌåÆ numbers from files. |
| **String to Byte Array** | `string` | `byte array` (U8) | Convert for serial port transmission. |
| **Byte Array to String** | `byte array` | `string` | Reconstruct after serial reception. |
| **Reverse String** | `string` | `reversed string` | Symmetry checks (palindromes). |
| **Rotate String** | `string`, `n` | `rotated string` | Shift characters cyclically. |
| **Upper/Lower Case** | `string` | `converted string` | Normalize user inputs. |
| **Trim Whitespace** | `string` | `trimmed string` | Clean files. |
| **Empty String Constant** | ŌĆö | `""` | Initialize. |

---

## 2.3 Editing, Formatting, and Parsing Strings

### A. Editing Strings
- **Replace Substring** ŌĆö replaces a portion of a string with another substring. Parameters: `string`, `offset` (0-indexed), `length` (number of characters to replace), and `substring` (replacement text).
- **Search and Replace String** ŌĆö global replacement of one substring with another throughout the entire string.

### B. Formatting Strings
- **Format Into String** converts numeric values to a string using *format specifiers*.
- Format specifiers follow the **C-language printf** conventions, plus LabVIEW-specific codes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LabVIEW Format Specifier Cheat-Sheet** (the single most-tested item in Module 3):

| Specifier | Meaning | Example Input | Output String |
|---|---|---|---|
| `%d` | Signed decimal integer | `42` | `"42"` |
| `%f` | Floating-point (6 decimals default) | `3.14159` | `"3.141590"` |
| `%.<n>f` | Floating-point with `n` decimals | `%.3f` of `3.14159` | `"3.142"` |
| `%e` | Scientific notation | `3.14e5` | `"3.140000e+05"` |
| `%g` | Auto %f or %e (shortest) | `0.0000001` | `"1.00000e-07"` |
| `%x` | Lowercase hex | `255` | `"ff"` |
| `%X` | Uppercase hex | `255` | `"FF"` |
| `%o` | Octal | `8` | `"10"` |
| `%b` | Binary | `5` | `"101"` |
| `%s` | String | `"Hi"` | `"Hi"` |
| `%c` | Single ASCII char (by code) | `65` | `"A"` |
| `%t` | Boolean (T/F) | `TRUE` | `"TRUE"` |
| `%%` | Literal percent sign | ŌĆö | `"%"` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Embedded Constants in Format String**: `%%` is used to insert a literal `%` sign. To embed a literal tab, newline, or carriage return, you right-click the format string and select **String Constant ŌåÆ ŌĆś\\ŌĆÖ Codes Display**, then enter `\t`, `\n`, `\r`.

### C. Parsing Strings
- **Scan From String** ŌĆö the inverse of Format Into String. It uses a **format string** to extract numbers/words from a string and convert them back to numeric data. It uses the `[offset]` output to continue scanning from the next position ŌĆö vital for reading multi-value text files (e.g., timestamp + value + unit).
- **Match Pattern** ŌĆö searches for a *regular expression* and returns the matching substring plus the rest. Often used for **CSV (Comma-Separated Values)** parsing.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Use ŌĆö DAQ Data Logging**
> A temperature sensor logging once per second:
> `Format Into String("T:%.2fC, RH:%.1f%%, %s", temp, humidity, timestamp)`
> Produces ŌåÆ `"T:23.45C, RH:54.3%, 2024-03-15 10:30:00"`
> The same string can be **scanned back** using `Scan From String` to recover the original numbers.

---

## 2.4 Configuring String Controls and Indicators

Three key properties on the **Properties dialog** (right-click the string ŌåÆ Properties):
1. **Display Style** ŌĆö Normal / ŌĆś\ŌĆÖ Codes / Password / Hex (see table above).
2. **Limit to Single Line** ŌĆö restricts entry to a single line; disables Enter key (default). **Disable** this for multi-line text logs.
3. **Update Value While Typing** ŌĆö if enabled, the control emits its value on every keystroke (useful for live search). If disabled, the value is committed only on **Enter** or when focus is lost.
4. **Visible Items** ŌĆö toggle scrollbar, word wrap, label, caption.
5. **Justification** ŌĆö Left / Center / Right text alignment.

---

## 2.5 Basics of File Input/Output (File I/O)

### Why File I/O?
- **Persistence** ŌĆö measurements survive power-off.
- **Data Sharing** ŌĆö ASCII files can be opened in Excel, MATLAB, Python.
- **Configuration Storage** ŌĆö load calibration constants at startup.
- **Report Generation** ŌĆö automated test reports (`.txt`, `.csv`, `.xlsx`).

### The Three-Step Pattern (Universal Law of File I/O)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Open ŌåÆ Read/Write ŌåÆ Close** ŌĆö ALL File I/O in LabVIEW follows this pattern. Failing to **Close** a file leads to:
> 1. **Buffer Loss** ŌĆö data stuck in the RAM write buffer never reaches the disk.
> 2. **Resource Leak** ŌĆö refnum is not released, locking the file.
> 3. **File Corruption** ŌĆö interrupted writes leave a partial file.

> Always use the **Simple Error Handler** or the error cluster wiring to detect and propagate errors at every stage.

---

## 2.6 Choosing a File I/O Format ŌĆö The Master Decision Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the highest-weight KTU Module 3 question** ŌĆö examiners love asking *"When would you choose ASCII over Binary, and why?"*

| Format | Extension | Readability | Size | Speed | Precision | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ASCII Text** | `.txt` | Ō£ģ Human | ŌØī Large | ŌØī Slow | ŌØī Approx (decimals) | Reports, config files, debugging. |
| **CSV** | `.csv` | Ō£ģ Human | ŌØī Large | ŌØī Slow | ŌØī Approx | Excel interchange, DAQ logs. |
| **LVM** | `.lvm` | Ō£ģ Human | ŌØī Large | ŌØī Slow | ŌØī Approx | NI-specific; auto-plottable. |
| **Binary** | `.dat`, `.bin` | ŌØī Raw bytes | Ō£ģ Small | Ō£ģ Fast | Ō£ģ Full U64 / DBL | High-speed DAQ, large arrays. |
| **TDMS** | `.tdms` | ŌØī (but indexed) | Ō£ģ Compact | Ō£ģ Very Fast | Ō£ģ Full | **National Instruments standard** ŌĆö recommended for high-throughput logging. |
| **Waveform** | `.wfm` | ŌØī | Ō£ģ Small | Ō£ģ Fast | Ō£ģ Full | Single waveform with metadata. |
| **Excel** | `.xlsx` | Ō£ģ (via Report Toolkit) | ŌØī Large | ŌØī Slow | ŌØī Approx | Office reports, formatted tables. |

**Trade-off Summary:**
- **ASCII (Text/CSV/LVM)** ŌĆö *Human-friendly, portable, slow, large.* Use for **final reports** or **small data sets**.
- **Binary (TDMS/DAT)** ŌĆö *Machine-optimized, fast, compact, full precision.* Use for **high-speed DAQ** and **long-duration logs**.

---

## 2.7 LabVIEW Data Directory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The **LabVIEW Data Directory** is a designated folder where LabVIEW stores the **default `VIHistory`**, **default data values** (saved with `Operate ŌåÆ Make Current Values Default`), and acts as the *working directory* for File I/O VIs when **no absolute path** is provided.

- The default location is: `C:\Users\<username>\Documents\LabVIEW Data\`
- Configurable via: `Tools ŌåÆ Options ŌåÆ Paths ŌåÆ Default Data Directory`
- When a VI uses a **relative path**, the path is **resolved relative to the LabVIEW Data Directory** (or the directory containing the VI, depending on context).

---

## 2.8 File I/O VIs ŌĆö The Complete Catalogue

### High-Level VIs (Simple ŌĆö recommended for beginners)
| VI | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Write to Spreadsheet File** | Writes 1D/2D array ŌåÆ text file (delimiter = Tab by default). |
| **Read from Spreadsheet File** | Reads delimited text ŌåÆ 1D/2D array. |
| **Write to Text File** | Writes string ŌåÆ text file (append or replace). |
| **Read from Text File** | Reads entire text file ŌåÆ string. |
| **Write to Binary File** | Writes any data type ŌåÆ binary file. |
| **Read from Binary File** | Reads binary data back as specified type. |
| **Write to Measurement File** | Writes waveforms/data + headers ŌåÆ LVM or TDMS. |
| **Read from Measurement File** | Reads LVM/TDMS files. |

### Low-Level VIs (Full control)
| VI | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Open/Create/Replace File** | Opens existing or creates new; returns refnum. |
| **Read File** | Reads N bytes at current position. |
| **Write File** | Writes N bytes at current position. |
| **Close File** | Flushes buffer, releases refnum. |
| **Set File Position** | Moves the read/write pointer. |
| **Get File Position** | Returns current pointer position. |
| **File/Directory Info** | Returns size, timestamps, attributes. |
| **List Folder** | Enumerates files in a directory. |
| **Move/Copy/Delete File** | File system management. |
| **Create Folder** | Make new directory. |

---

## 2.9 Creating a Relative Path

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A **Relative Path** is a file path expressed *relative* to a known base directory (the **VI's location** or the **LabVIEW Data Directory**). It enables VIs to be **moved between computers** without breaking the file links.

### LabVIEW Mechanism ŌĆö The Strip Path + Build Path Pattern

1. **Strip Path** (Programming ŌåÆ File I/O ŌåÆ Advanced File Functions) ŌĆö given an absolute path to *any* file inside a target folder, returns **just the folder part**.
2. **Build Path** ŌĆö concatenates a base folder with a sub-path to form a complete path.
3. Combine: `Strip Path of <current VI's path>` ŌåÆ `Build Path with "data\\log.txt"` ŌåÆ **portable absolute path**.

### Alternative: Use the `Application Directory` Function
- `Application Directory` returns the folder containing the **current VI**.
- `Build Path` then appends the relative sub-path.
- Result: a **portable** reference that works on any PC.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip ŌĆö Why use relative paths?**
> 1. **Portability** ŌĆö VI folder can be zipped and emailed; it works on any PC.
> 2. **Security** ŌĆö avoids hard-coded `C:\...` paths that vary by user.
> 3. **Version control friendly** ŌĆö Git/SVN merge cleanly without absolute paths.
> 4. **Sandbox** ŌĆö entire project can be moved to a different drive letter.

### Relative Path Tokens (LV 2010+)

| Token | Resolves To |
|---|---|
| `<vilib>` | Folder containing the current VI. |
| `<userlib>` | `C:\Users\<user>\Documents\LabVIEW Data\user.lib` |
| `<resource>` | The VI's resource folder (LLB or `*.vit`). |
| `<not a path>` | A literal flag to disable path resolution. |

These are entered in the **Browse Options** dialog when using the file dialog box.

---

## 2.10 KTU High-Yield Master Formula & Syntax Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No mathematical formulas in the strict sense apply to File I/O, but the following "syntax rules" are equivalent ŌĆö they are the equations a student must memorize.**

| # | Rule | Expression / Pattern | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Universal File I/O sequence | $\text{Open} \rightarrow \text{R/W} \rightarrow \text{Close}$ | All file operations. |
| 2 | File path concatenation | $P_{\text{final}} = P_{\text{base}} \,\|\, P_{\text{rel}}$ | Building portable paths. |
| 3 | ASCII size of a number | $N_{\text{bytes}} \approx \lceil \log_{10}(x) \rceil + 1$ | Estimating log file size. |
| 4 | Format string length | $L_{out} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} L_{\text{spec}_i}$ | Buffer pre-allocation. |
| 5 | Match Pattern offset advance | $\text{offset}_{n+1} = \text{offset}_n + L_{\text{match}}$ | Multi-token CSV parsing. |
| 6 | LabVIEW Data Directory | $P_{data} = \text{`C:\\Users\\<u>\\Documents\\LabVIEW Data`}$ | Default storage. |
| 7 | Application Directory | $P_{app} = \text{Folder}(\text{current VI})$ | Relative-path root. |
| 8 | Buffer flush trigger | $\text{Close}() \Rightarrow \text{Write} \rightarrow \text{Disk}$ | Avoiding data loss. |
| 9 | Refnum scope rule | $\text{Open} \leftrightarrow \text{Close in same VI}$ | Resource leak prevention. |
| 10 | Hex Ōåö String | $\text{byte}_{16} = \text{ASCII}(n), \quad n \in [0, 255]$ | Serial port debugging. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example ŌĆö Formatting a Multi-Channel DAQ Log Line

**Problem (KTU-Style):**
A VI measures three quantities from a strain gauge DAQ system:
- Channel 1: `strain` in microstrain (╬╝╔ø), type = **DBL**, value = `1234.5678`
- Channel 2: `load_N` in Newtons, type = **DBL**, value = `98765.432`
- Channel 3: `is_valid` flag, type = **Boolean**, value = `TRUE`

Design a LabVIEW string (using **Format Into String**) that produces a single, comma-separated line suitable for a CSV log file, with the strain shown to **2 decimals**, load to **integer**, and the boolean as `T/F`.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the format string template:**
A CSV row needs: `value1,value2,value3\n`
The format specifiers for each are:
- Strain: `%.2f` (two decimals)
- Load: `%.0f` (zero decimals ŌåÆ integer)
- Boolean: `%t` (T/F)
- Separator: `,`
- Newline: `\n`

So the **format string** is:
```
"%.2f,%.0f,%t\n"
```

**Step 2 ŌĆö Wire inputs in order:**

$$
\text{Input}_{1} = \text{strain} \ (\text{DBL}) = 1234.5678
$$
$$
\text{Input}_{2} = \text{load\_N} \ (\text{DBL}) = 98765.432
$$
$$
\text{Input}_{3} = \text{is\_valid} \ (\text{Boolean}) = \text{TRUE}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply each specifier:**

For `%.2f` applied to 1234.5678:
$$\text{format}(1234.5678, \texttt{"\%.2f"}) = \texttt{"1234.57"} \quad \text{(rounded half-up)}$$

For `%.0f` applied to 98765.432:
$$\text{format}(98765.432, \texttt{"\%.0f"}) = \texttt{"98765"} \quad \text{(rounded to integer)}$$

For `%t` applied to `TRUE`:
$$\text{format}(\text{TRUE}, \texttt{"\%t"}) = \texttt{"TRUE"} \quad \text{(default is full word)}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Concatenate with format string:**
$$\text{Output} = \underbrace{\texttt{"1234.57"}}_{\text{Input 1}} + \underbrace{\texttt{","}}_{\text{literal}} + \underbrace{\texttt{"98765"}}_{\text{Input 2}} + \underbrace{\texttt{","}}_{\text{literal}} + \underbrace{\texttt{"TRUE"}}_{\text{Input 3}} + \underbrace{\texttt{"\textbackslash n"}}_{\text{newline}}$$

**Final Resulting String:**
```
1234.57,98765,TRUE
```
followed by a newline character (`\n`, ASCII 10) ready to be written to file.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example ŌĆö Parsing the CSV Line Back

**Problem:** Read the string above from disk and reconstruct the three numeric values using **Scan From String**.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the scan format string** (must mirror the format used to write):
```
"%.2f,%.0f,%t"
```

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Scan From String:**

Given input string `s = "1234.57,98765,TRUE"`, the scan function reads sequentially:
- Reads up to `,` ŌåÆ matches `1234.57` ŌåÆ converts to DBL: `1234.57`
- Skips the `,`
- Reads up to `,` ŌåÆ matches `98765` ŌåÆ converts to DBL: `98765.0`
- Skips the `,`
- Reads `TRUE` ŌåÆ converts to Boolean: `TRUE`

The `[offset]` output reports position `len(s) = 16`, indicating the entire string was consumed (no parse error).

> [!NOTE]
> **Robustness Tip:** To tolerate optional whitespace or unit suffixes (e.g., `╬╝╔ø`, `N`), insert `%s` placeholders in the format string to *skip* the unknown token: `"%.2f╬╝╔ø,%.0fN,%t"`.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö String Function Selection Guide

| Goal | Recommended Function | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Build a log filename from a counter | **Concatenate Strings** | Fast, simple, multiple inputs. |
| Extract just the file extension | **Match Pattern** with `*\.` regex | Returns last segment. |
| Convert a temperature reading to display | **Format Into String** | Numeric ŌåÆ ASCII for display. |
| Parse a serial port response | **Scan From String** | ASCII ŌåÆ numeric. |
| Anonymize a password field | **String Control in Password Display** | Hides characters. |
| Inspect raw bytes of a packet | **String Indicator in Hex Display** | Shows U8 values. |
| Insert a tab-separated column | **Format Into String** with `\t` | CSV/TDF output. |
| Build a robust file path | **Strip Path** + **Build Path** | Portability. |
| Check if a string contains "ERROR" | **Match Pattern** (search mode) | Boolean match output. |
| Replace "OLD" with "NEW" everywhere | **Search and Replace String** | Single call, global. |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step ŌĆö Building a Portable File Path

**Problem:** A VI must log to `.\logs\sensor_data.csv` where `.` is the folder containing the VI.

**Step 1:** Place **Application Directory** VI on the block diagram. It returns:
$$P_{app} = \text{"C:\textbackslash Projects\textbackslash DAQ\_Logger\"}$$

**Step 2:** Place **Build Path** VI. Wire:
$$P_{app} \rightarrow \text{base path}$$
$$\text{"logs"} \rightarrow \text{name or relative path}$$

Output: $P_1 = \text{"C:\textbackslash Projects\textbackslash DAQ\_Logger\textbackslash logs"}$

**Step 3:** Place a **second** Build Path (LabVIEW allows nesting). Wire:
$$P_1 \rightarrow \text{base path}$$
$$\text{"sensor\_data.csv"} \rightarrow \text{name or relative path}$$

Final: $P_{final} = \text{"C:\textbackslash Projects\textbackslash DAQ\_Logger\textbackslash logs\textbackslash sensor\_data.csv"}$

**Step 4:** Use this $P_{final}$ as input to **Open/Create/Replace File** ŌåÆ **Write to Text File** ŌåÆ **Close File**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation Check:** If the `logs` folder does not exist, the Write VI returns **Error 7** ("File not found"). Always include a `Create Folder` step wrapped in a **Case Structure** with `error status ŌåÆ True` to ensure the directory exists.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## ­¤ōØ PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: List any four display modes of a String Indicator in LabVIEW and state the purpose of *Hex Display* mode.**

**Model Answer:**
The four display modes of a string indicator in LabVIEW are:
1. **Normal Display** ŌĆö shows printable ASCII characters (default).
2. **ŌĆś\ŌĆÖ Codes Display** ŌĆö displays non-printable characters using backslash escape codes (e.g., `\n`).
3. **Password Display** ŌĆö masks every character with a bullet/asterisk for security.
4. **Hex Display** ŌĆö shows the raw **U8 byte values** in hexadecimal (e.g., `48 65 6C 6C 6F`).

**Purpose of Hex Display:** It is used by developers to **inspect the raw byte content** of a string during debugging, especially when verifying **binary protocols** sent/received over **serial (RS-232)**, **GPIB**, or **VISA** ports. It is essential when troubleshooting non-printable control characters (STX, ETX, CR, LF) that the Normal display would hide.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between ASCII and Binary file formats in LabVIEW. State one engineering scenario where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | ASCII Format | Binary Format |
|---|---|---|
| **Readability** | Human-readable; can be opened in Notepad/Excel. | Not human-readable; requires LabVIEW/MATLAB. |
| **File Size** | Large (one byte per digit + delimiters). | Compact (raw bits of the data type). |
| **Precision** | Limited (decimals truncated for display). | Full (preserves U64, DBL, I32, etc.). |
| **Speed** | Slower (ASCII conversion overhead). | Faster (no conversion). |
| **Preferred Scenario** | **Final lab report** of a temperature profile saved for inspection. | **High-speed DAQ stream** of 1 MHz sampling over 24 hours. |

---

## ­¤ōØ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer **one full question** (with both sub-parts) out of the internal choice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Sample Paper]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-step universal procedure for File I/O** in LabVIEW. List the **High-Level** and **Low-Level File I/O VIs** from the Functions palette and explain the role of the **refnum** (7 marks).

**(b)** A VI records the temperature (in ┬░C) and pressure (in kPa) from a process plant every second. Design the **block diagram logic** to:
- (i) Format a comma-separated string log line.
- (ii) Write it to `C:\PlantLogs\data.csv`.
- (iii) Make the path portable using **Application Directory** + **Build Path**.
- (iv) Explain the use of **Format Into String** with appropriate specifiers (7 marks).

---

#### **Model Answer for Q.A(a):**

**The Three-Step Procedure:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Open / Create / Replace:**
- Use `Open/Create/Replace File.vi` (`Programming ŌåÆ File I/O`).
- Specify the file path and access mode (`read-only`, `write-only`, `read/write`).
- The VI returns a **refnum** (file reference number) and an error cluster.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Read or Write:**
- For text: `Write to Text File.vi` or `Read from Text File.vi`.
- For binary: `Write File.vi` or `Read File.vi`.
- The refnum from Step 1 is wired as the file input.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Close:**
- Use `Close File.vi` to **flush the write buffer to disk** and release the refnum.
- **Failing to close causes data loss** because data sits in the RAM buffer.

**Refnum:** A refnum is a unique identifier (pointer) that LabVIEW assigns to an open file. It is passed between Open, Read/Write, and Close VIs to ensure the correct file is being operated on, similar to a library card number. Refnums must be closed to avoid memory leaks.

**High-Level VIs:** Write to Spreadsheet File, Read from Spreadsheet File, Write to Text File, Read from Text File, Write to Binary File, Read from Binary File, Write to Measurement File, Read from Measurement File.

**Low-Level VIs:** Open/Create/Replace File, Read File, Write File, Close File, Set File Position, Get File Position, File/Directory Info, List Folder, Create Folder, Move/Copy/Delete File.

---

#### **Model Answer for Q.A(b):**

**Block Diagram Logic:**

```
[While Loop i=0..N]
   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ[Temperature (DBL)]ŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ[Pressure (DBL)]ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö╝ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║[Format Into String: "%.2f,%.2f\n"]ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║[String]
   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ[i (I32) for index]ŌöĆŌöĆŌöś                                          Ōöé
                                                                       Ō¢╝
[Application Directory]ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║[Build Path: "logs"]ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║[Build Path: "data.csv"]ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║[Path]
                                                                       Ōöé
                                                                       Ō¢╝
[Open/Create/Replace File: write-only or append]ŌöĆŌöĆ(refnum)ŌöĆŌ¢║[Write to Text File]ŌöĆŌöĆ(refnum)ŌöĆŌ¢║[Close File]
```

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**(i) Format Into String Logic:**
Format string: `"%.2f,%.2f\n"`
- Input 1 = Temperature (DBL)
- Input 2 = Pressure (DBL)
- Example output: `"23.45,101.32\n"`

**(ii) Writing to file:**
Use `Open/Create/Replace File.vi` with access = `write-only` and the path wired from step (iii). Pipe the formatted string into `Write to Text File.vi`. Wire the refnum output of the open VI to the refnum input of the write VI.

**(iii) Making path portable:**
- `Application Directory` returns the folder containing the VI (e.g., `C:\PlantLogs\`).
- `Build Path` with `name = "data.csv"` produces `C:\PlantLogs\data.csv`.
- If the user moves the entire folder, the VI still works on any PC.

**(iv) Specifier Justification:**
- `%.2f` for temperature (2-decimal precision) and pressure (2 decimals) ŌĆö provides reasonable engineering precision without bloating the log.
- `,` is the literal CSV separator.
- `\n` inserts a newline so each sample appears on a new line in the file.
