<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­¤ōś MODULE 4 ŌĆö DATA ACQUISITION (DAQ) SYSTEMS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Data Acquisition (DAQ) ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Acquisition (DAQ)** is the process of sampling real-world physical phenomena (temperature, pressure, flow, strain, light intensity, etc.), converting them into electrical signals using **transducers**, processing them through **signal conditioning** circuits, and digitizing them via an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** so that a computer or embedded controller can store, analyze, and display the data.

In the context of **Virtual Instrumentation (PEICT745)**, a DAQ system acts as the **"senses and nerves"** of a LabVIEW-based virtual instrument ŌĆö it bridges the physical world with the software-defined analytical world.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy ŌĆö The Five Human Senses as a DAQ System**
> 
> Imagine a human body trying to understand its environment:
> - ­¤æü’ĖÅ **Eyes, Ears, Skin (Receptors)** = **Transducers** (convert light, sound, pressure into electrical nerve impulses).
> - ­¤¦Ā **Spinal Cord & Brainstem (Pre-processing)** = **Signal Conditioning** (amplifies weak signals, filters noise, isolates dangerous voltages).
> - ­¤¦Ā **Cerebral Cortex (Digitization & Cognition)** = **ADC + DAQ Software** (samples nerve signals at discrete times, stores, and interprets them).
> - ­¤Æ¼ **Speech/Motor Output (Reaction)** = **Analog Output / Digital I/O** (drives a speaker, motor, or LED).
> 
> A DAQ system is, in essence, an **electronic nervous system** for a measurement instrument.

---

### 1.3 Transducers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transducer** ŌĆö A device that converts one form of energy (physical, chemical, mechanical) into an electrical signal (voltage, current, or frequency). It is the **front-end sensor** of any DAQ system.

**Two broad classifications:**

| Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Active Transducer** | Generates its own electrical output (no external power needed) | Thermocouple, Photovoltaic cell, Piezoelectric sensor |
| **Passive Transducer** | Requires external excitation (voltage/current source) | RTD, Thermistor, Strain gauge, LVDT |

**Energy-domain classification:**

- **Input Transducer (Sensor):** Physical ŌåÆ Electrical (e.g., thermocouple: heat ŌåÆ mV)
- **Output Transducer (Actuator):** Electrical ŌåÆ Physical (e.g., speaker: V ŌåÆ sound wave)

---

### 1.4 Signals

> [!NOTE]
> A **Signal** is a physical quantity that varies with time, space, or another independent variable, carrying information.

**Taxonomy of Signals in DAQ:**

1. **Analog Signal** ŌĆö Continuous in both **time** and **amplitude**. Example: voltage from a thermocouple.
2. **Digital Signal** ŌĆö Discrete in both time and amplitude (binary 0/1). Example: switch status.
3. **Discrete-Time Signal** ŌĆö Continuous amplitude, sampled at discrete time intervals. Example: ADC output.
4. **Continuous Signal** ŌĆö Defined for every instant of time.

**Key Signal Metrics (always bold for KTU):**

- **Amplitude** ŌĆö peak value of the signal (V or A).
- **Frequency (f)** ŌĆö cycles per second, measured in **Hz**.
- **Period (T)** ŌĆö $T = 1/f$ (seconds).
- **RMS Value** ŌĆö equivalent DC value: $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt}$.
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** ŌĆö measure of desired signal vs. noise floor (dB).

---

### 1.5 Signal Conditioning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Signal Conditioning** is the manipulation of a transducer's raw output signal to make it suitable for the ADC input range. Raw sensor signals are typically too small, noisy, non-linear, or laden with common-mode voltages to feed directly into an ADC.

**Standard signal conditioning operations:**

- **Amplification** ŌĆö boosts low-level signals (mV) to ADC range (e.g., 0ŌĆō10 V).
- **Filtering** ŌĆö removes unwanted frequency components (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass).
- **Isolation** ŌĆö galvanic separation using optocouplers or transformers; protects the PC and removes ground loops.
- **Linearization** ŌĆö corrects non-linear sensor characteristics (e.g., thermocouple mV-vs-┬░C table).
- **Excitation** ŌĆö provides the required power/voltage/current to passive sensors (e.g., 10 V for a strain gauge bridge).
- **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** ŌĆö for thermocouples.

---

### 1.6 Components of a Computer-Based Measurement System

> [!NOTE]
> A **Computer-Based Measurement System (CBMS)** combines a sensor interface, a DAQ device, signal conditioning, a host computer, and software to perform measurement and analysis that previously required standalone bench instruments (oscilloscope, multimeter, spectrum analyzer, etc.).

The standard **five-block architecture** is:

1. **Sensor / Transducer**
2. **Signal Conditioning Circuit**
3. **DAQ Hardware** (ADC, DAC, DIO, Counters)
4. **Host Computer (PC) with DAQ Driver and Application Software (LabVIEW)**
5. **Application / Display / Storage**

---

### 1.7 DAQ Hardware ŌĆö Top-Level View

A DAQ device (e.g., NI PCI-6221, NI USB-6009) typically contains:

- **Analog Inputs (AI)** ŌĆö Multiplexed ADC channels.
- **Analog Outputs (AO)** ŌĆö DAC channels.
- **Digital I/O (DIO) lines** ŌĆö for TTL/CMOS logic signaling.
- **Counter/Timers** ŌĆö for frequency, period, pulse-width, and event counting.
- **On-board memory / FIFO buffer** ŌĆö temporary storage between ADC and PC bus.
- **Bus interface** ŌĆö PCI, USB, PXI, Ethernet, or PCI Express.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Transducer Characteristics ŌĆö Mathematical Model

A transducer can be modeled as a **linear input-output** relation:

$$
y(t) = K \cdot x(t) + b
$$

where:
- $y(t)$ = electrical output (V, mA, Hz)
- $x(t)$ = physical input (┬░C, N, m, Pa)
- $K$ = **Sensitivity** (units: V/┬░C, mV/N, etc.)
- $b$ = **Offset / Bias**

> [!NOTE]
> For an **ideal transducer**, $K$ is constant over the entire input range. Real transducers exhibit **non-linearity**, **hysteresis**, and **drift** ŌĆö these are *systematic errors* the DAQ system must handle via calibration or linearization tables.

---

## 2.2 Signals ŌĆö Mathematical Form

### 2.2.1 Sinusoidal Signal (most fundamental test signal)

$$
x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)
$$

- $A$ = amplitude (V)
- $f$ = frequency (Hz)
- $\phi$ = phase (radians)

### 2.2.2 Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A bandlimited signal with maximum frequency component $f_{max}$ must be sampled at a rate **at least twice** $f_{max}$ to be perfectly reconstructed from its samples.

$$
f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}
$$

The minimum acceptable rate $f_s = 2f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**. Sampling below it causes **aliasing** ŌĆö false low-frequency artifacts that distort the measurement irreversibly.

### 2.2.3 Quantization & ADC Resolution

A unipolar **N-bit ADC** with input range $V_{FS}$ (Full Scale) has:

- **Number of discrete levels:** $L = 2^N$
- **Resolution (LSB weight):** $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$
- **Quantization Error (max):** $\pm\tfrac{1}{2}\Delta V = \pm\tfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{N+1}}$

### 2.2.4 Theoretical Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)

For a full-scale sine input:

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \cdot N + 1.76
$$

> Each additional bit of resolution adds approximately **6 dB** of dynamic range ŌĆö a foundational KTU number.

### 2.2.5 Throughput / Settling Time

For a multiplexed AI system with **n** channels and per-channel sampling rate $f_s$:

$$
f_{agg} = n \cdot f_s
$$

The **aggregate throughput** must not exceed the ADC's maximum conversion rate. If the ADC has an internal multiplexer settling time $t_{settle}$, the practical per-channel rate is:

$$
f_{s,\text{per-channel}} \leq \frac{1}{t_{settle} + t_{conv}}
$$

### 2.2.6 Digital I/O (DIO) Logic Levels

Standard **TTL** levels (most common in KTU practicals):

| Logic Level | Output Voltage | Input Voltage Recognized As |
|---|---|---|
| **LOW (0)** | $\leq 0.4$ V | $\leq 0.8$ V |
| **HIGH (1)** | $\geq 2.4$ V | $\geq 2.0$ V |

Sink/Source current is typically $\pm$**24 mA** per line.

### 2.2.7 Counter / Timer Frequency Measurement

For a counter measuring a pulse train of frequency $f$ using a **gate time** $T_g$:

$$
f = \frac{N_{counts}}{T_g}
$$

Resolution improves with longer $T_g$:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_g} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Resolution} = \frac{\Delta f}{f} = \frac{1}{N_{counts}}
$$

---

## 2.3 Signal Conditioning ŌĆö Operational Equations

### 2.3.1 Instrumentation Amplifier Gain

The classic three-op-amp **instrumentation amplifier** (used in nearly every DAQ front-end):

$$
V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2} \cdot (V_+ - V_-)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The high **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** (often >100 dB) is what makes the instrumentation amplifier ideal for extracting small differential signals riding on large common-mode voltages ŌĆö exactly the case with strain gauges and thermocouples in industrial environments.

### 2.3.2 Low-Pass RC Filter Cutoff

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}
$$

### 2.3.3 Thermocouple Sensitivity (Type K)

$$
V \approx 41 \, \mu V / ┬░C \quad \text{at } 25┬░\text{C}
$$

This tiny signal **must** be amplified before reaching the ADC ŌĆö a textbook signal-conditioning example.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Master Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sampling Rate (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2f_{max}$ | Hz | Avoid aliasing |
| 2 | ADC Resolution (LSB) | $\Delta V = V_{FS}/2^N$ | V | **N = bits** |
| 3 | Quantization Error | $\pm V_{FS}/2^{N+1}$ | V | Half-LSB |
| 4 | SQNR (sine input) | $6.02N + 1.76$ | dB | 6 dB per bit |
| 5 | Aggregate Throughput | $f_{agg} = n \cdot f_s$ | samples/s | **n** = channels |
| 6 | Transducer Linear Model | $y = Kx + b$ | varies | K = sensitivity |
| 7 | RMS Value | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{T}\int v^2 dt}$ | V | DC equivalent |
| 8 | Period / Frequency | $T = 1/f$ | s / Hz | Reciprocal |
| 9 | Instrumentation Amp Gain | $G = (1+2R_1/R_g)(R_3/R_2)$ | unitless | CMRR high |
| 10 | LPF Cutoff | $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ | Hz |  ŌłÆ3 dB point |
| 11 | Counter Frequency | $f = N_{counts}/T_g$ | Hz | Gate method |
| 12 | Thermocouple Sensitivity | Ōēł 41 ╬╝V/┬░C (Type K) | ╬╝V/┬░C | Needs CJC |
| 13 | TTL Logic HIGH | Ōēź 2.4 V (out), Ōēź 2.0 V (in) | V | DIO standard |
| 14 | TTL Logic LOW | Ōēż 0.4 V (out), Ōēż 0.8 V (in) | V | DIO standard |
| 15 | Buffer / FIFO Use | prevents sample loss | ŌĆö | Bus-latency hiding |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** Memorize rows 1ŌĆō4 and 9ŌĆō11 ŌĆö these are the most frequently asked 3-mark and 14-mark formulas in ESE Module-4 questions.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation ŌĆö ADC Resolution and Quantization Error

**Problem context:** A 12-bit ADC is used in a DAQ system with input range $\pm 10$ V. Determine (a) the LSB weight, (b) the maximum quantization error, and (c) the theoretical SQNR.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Identify parameters

- Number of bits $N = 12$
- Full-scale range $V_{FS} = 20$ V (since $\pm 10$ V ŌåÆ peak-to-peak = 20 V)

### Step 2 ŌĆö Compute number of discrete levels

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 2^N \\
  &= 2^{12} \\
  &= 4096 \text{ discrete levels}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Compute LSB (resolution)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} \\
         &= \frac{20}{4096} \\
         &= 4.8828 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} \\
         &= 4.883 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

So each bit "step" represents **4.883 mV**.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Maximum quantization error

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_q &= \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2} \\
    &= \pm \frac{4.883 \text{ mV}}{2} \\
    &= \pm 2.4414 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Theoretical SQNR

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SQNR}_{dB} &= 6.02 N + 1.76 \\
                 &= 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 \\
                 &= 72.24 + 1.76 \\
                 &= 74.0 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 12-bit ADC has roughly 74 dB of dynamic range ŌĆö each additional bit adds ~6 dB.

---

## 3.2 Derivation ŌĆö Sampling Rate and Anti-Aliasing Filter

**Problem context:** A vibration sensor on a motor produces signals with maximum meaningful frequency $f_{max} = 5$ kHz. Determine the minimum sampling rate and the cutoff frequency of the anti-aliasing low-pass filter.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Nyquist criterion

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_s \geq 2 f_{max} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_s &\geq 2 \times 5000 \\
f_s &\geq 10{,}000 \text{ samples/s} = 10 \text{ kS/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Choose practical oversampling (KTU standard = 5├ŚŌĆō10├Ś)

$$
f_{s,\text{practical}} = 10 \times f_{max} = 50 \text{ kS/s}
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Design the anti-aliasing filter

The filter's cutoff $f_c$ should be just above $f_{max}$ but well below $f_s/2$:

$$
f_{max} < f_c < \frac{f_s}{2}
$$

For 1st-order RC:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_c &= \frac{1}{2\pi R C} \\
\text{Choose } C = 10 \text{ nF}, \text{ then } R &= \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $f_c = 5.5$ kHz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 5500 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}} \\
  &= \frac{1}{3.456 \times 10^{-4}} \\
  &= 2.89 \text{ k}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation ŌĆö Counter-Based Frequency Measurement

**Problem context:** A square wave of unknown frequency is fed to a counter. With a 1-second gate time, 10,000 counts are accumulated. Determine the input frequency and the measurement resolution.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Frequency computation

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{N_{counts}}{T_g} \\
  &= \frac{10{,}000}{1} \\
  &= 10{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 10 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Resolution (1-count ambiguity)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta f &= \frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{1}{1} = 1 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Relative resolution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta f}{f} &= \frac{1}{10{,}000} = 0.01\% = 100 \text{ ppm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Improvement by extending gate

For a 10-second gate:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1 \text{ Hz}, \quad \text{rel. res.} = 0.001\%
$$

**Engineering insight:** Longer gate time ŌåÆ better resolution, but slower update rate. A classic KTU trade-off question.

---

## 3.4 Derivation ŌĆö Multiplexed DAQ Aggregate Throughput

**Problem context:** A DAQ board has 8 analog input channels multiplexed into a single ADC rated at 100 kS/s. The on-board multiplexer has a 2 ╬╝s settling time. Compute the maximum per-channel sampling rate.

### Step 1 ŌĆö ADC conversion time (assumes single conversion = 10 ╬╝s)

$$
t_{conv} = \frac{1}{f_{ADC}} = \frac{1}{100{,}000} = 10 \, \mu s
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Total per-channel period

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{ch} &= t_{settle} + t_{conv} \\
       &= 2 \, \mu s + 10 \, \mu s \\
       &= 12 \, \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Per-channel sample rate

$$
f_{s,ch} = \frac{1}{T_{ch}} = \frac{1}{12 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 83.33 \text{ kS/s}
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Aggregate throughput

$$
f_{agg} = 8 \times 83.33 = 666.67 \text{ kS/s}
$$

But the ADC max is 100 kS/s ŌćÆ the **bottleneck** is the ADC.

**Actual achievable per-channel rate:**

$$
f_{s,ch,\text{real}} = \frac{100{,}000}{8} = 12.5 \text{ kS/s per channel}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **simultaneous-sampling DAQ cards** (with one ADC per channel) are used for phase-critical applications like power analysis.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Choosing the Right DAQ Configuration

| Application | Sensor | Required Conditioning | DAQ Channel Type | Sampling Rate | KTU Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Room temperature logging | Thermistor / RTD | Excitation + linearization + LPF | Single-ended AI | 1 Hz | Slow physical variable |
| Engine vibration | Accelerometer (IEPE) | IEPE current source + AC coupling | Differential AI | Ōēź 20 kHz | $f_{max}$ Ōēł 5 kHz, Nyquist + margin |
| Strain gauge on beam | Wheatstone bridge | Excitation (5 V/10 V) + Inst. Amp | Differential AI | 100 Hz | DC / quasi-static |
| Heart rate (ECG) | Ag/AgCl electrodes | Inst. Amp (CMRR > 90 dB) + 50 Hz notch | Differential AI | 500 Hz | Mains interference rejection |
| Motor RPM | Optical encoder | Schmitt trigger squaring | Counter input | N/A | Frequency measurement |
| LED indicator | LED | Current-limiting resistor | DIO line | N/A | Static digital write |
| Audio playback | DAC | Reconstruction filter (LPF) | AO channel | Ōēź 44.1 kHz | Audio bandwidth |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hot-Spot:** This exact "application ŌåÆ DAQ configuration mapping" appears in nearly every ESE ŌĆö memorize the logic, not the table.

## 4.3 Flow Diagram ŌĆö NI-DAQmx Task Configuration Lifecycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **task** is the central abstraction in NI-DAQmx. Every AI/AO/DIO/Counter operation is a **task** with **virtual channels** inside it. Understanding this flow is essential for KTU LabVIEW-based VIs.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CreateTask
    CreateTask --> AddChannel: Add Virtual Channel
    AddChannel --> ConfigureTiming: Sample Clock and Rate
    ConfigureTiming --> ConfigureTrigger: Start Trigger Reference
    ConfigureTrigger --> StartTask: Begin Acquisition or Generation
    StartTask --> ReadWrite: Read AI or Write AO or DIO
    ReadWrite --> StopTask: User or Error Event
    StopTask --> ClearTask: Release Resources
    ClearTask --> [*]
    ReadWrite --> ReadWrite: Continuous Mode Loop
    StopTask --> ReadWrite: Restart if Continuous
```

**Stage descriptions:**

1. **Create Task** ŌĆö Allocate a logical container.
2. **Add Virtual Channel** ŌĆö Define physical channel + measurement type (voltage, current, freq, etc.).
3. **Configure Timing** ŌĆö Sample clock source, rate, number of samples, mode (finite/continuous/HW-timed).
4. **Configure Trigger** ŌĆö Optional start trigger (digital edge, analog threshold, etc.).
5. **Start Task** ŌĆö Arm hardware, begin streaming.
6. **Read / Write** ŌĆö Transfer samples between DAQ FIFO and PC memory.
7. **Stop + Clear Task** ŌĆö Always release resources to avoid memory leaks (a common KTU LabVIEW bug).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ­¤ōØ PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a transducer. Differentiate between active and passive transducers with two examples each.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **transducer** is a device that converts one form of energy into another, typically converting a physical quantity (temperature, pressure, force) into an electrical signal (voltage, current, frequency) suitable for measurement by a DAQ system.
> 
> | Type | Definition | Examples |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Active Transducer** | Generates electrical output without external power | Thermocouple, Piezoelectric crystal, Photovoltaic cell |
> | **Passive Transducer** | Requires external excitation (voltage/current) | Strain gauge, RTD, Thermistor, LVDT |
> 
> **Key distinction:** Active = self-generating (energy conversion). Passive = modulating element (energy control). **(1 mark ŌĆö definition, 1 mark ŌĆö active, 1 mark ŌĆö passive + examples)**

---

### **Q2. State the Nyquist Sampling Theorem. What is aliasing and how is it prevented?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** A bandlimited analog signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be completely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling rate satisfies
> 
> $$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$
> 
> The minimum rate $2 f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**.
> 
> **Aliasing:** When $f_s < 2 f_{max}$, high-frequency components fold back into the lower spectrum as false low-frequency signals, corrupting the measurement irreversibly.
> 
> **Prevention methods:**
> 1. Sample at $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (with safety margin of 5├ŚŌĆō10├Ś).
> 2. Use an **analog anti-aliasing low-pass filter** before the ADC with cutoff $f_c$ slightly above $f_{max}$.
> 
> **(1 mark ŌĆö theorem, 1 mark ŌĆö aliasing explanation, 1 mark ŌĆö prevention)**

---

## ­¤ōØ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE Module-4 offers an **internal choice** between two 14-mark questions. Each is split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### ŌŁÉ QUESTION A (14 Marks) ŌĆö DAQ Hardware & Architecture

#### **Q.A(a)** Explain the functional block diagram of a Data Acquisition (DAQ) system. List the major components and their roles. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A computer-based Data Acquisition system converts real-world physical phenomena into digital data for processing. The functional blocks are:

1. **Sensor / Transducer** (1 mark)
   - Converts physical quantity (temperature, pressure, strain) into a proportional electrical signal.
   - Examples: Thermocouple (heat ŌåÆ mV), Strain gauge (force ŌåÆ ╬öR).

2. **Signal Conditioning Circuit** (1.5 marks)
   - **Amplification:** Boosts millivolt-level signals to ADC's 0ŌĆō10 V range using an instrumentation amplifier (high CMRR > 100 dB).
   - **Filtering:** Low-pass, high-pass, band-pass filters remove noise; anti-aliasing LPF is critical.
   - **Isolation:** Optocoupler/transformer isolates PC from field-side high voltages; eliminates ground loops.
   - **Excitation:** Provides power to passive sensors (e.g., 5 V bridge excitation for strain gauge).
   - **Linearization:** Software/hardware correction of non-linear sensor curves (e.g., thermocouple polynomial).

3. **DAQ Hardware** (1.5 marks)
   - **Multiplexer (MUX):** Routes multiple AI channels to one ADC ŌĆö saves cost but reduces per-channel rate.
   - **Sample-and-Hold (S/H):** Freezes the analog voltage during ADC conversion; prevents aperture error.
   - **ADC (N-bit):** Converts analog ŌåÆ digital codes. Resolution = $V_{FS}/2^N$.
   - **FIFO Buffer:** Buffers data during PC bus latency, prevents sample loss.
   - **DAC, DIO ports, Counters** for output and digital/ timing functions.

4. **Host Computer + DAQ Driver** (1.5 marks)
   - **NI-DAQmx driver** provides low-level register access and API.
   - **DAQ Assistant / LabVIEW** provides high-level programming interface.
   - **Application software** performs display, logging, analysis (FFT, statistics), and control.

5. **Display / Storage** (1 mark)
   - Virtual front panel, file I/O (TDMS/LVM), network sharing, cloud upload.

**Block flow:**

$$
\text{Sensor} \to \text{Conditioning} \to \text{S/H} \to \text{MUX} \to \text{ADC} \to \text{FIFO} \to \text{Driver} \to \text{Software}
$$

---

#### **Q.A(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of Analog Input (AI) subsystem of a DAQ card. Differentiate between single-ended and differential analog input configurations. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**AI Subsystem Block Diagram (3 marks):**

$$
\text{AI Ch 0 to n} \to \text{MUX} \to \text{PGA} \to \text{S/H} \to \text{ADC} \to \text{FIFO} \to \text{Driver}
$$

- **Multiplexer:** Time-shares one ADC across N channels.
- **Programmable Gain Amplifier (PGA):** Selectable gain (1, 2, 5, 10, ŌĆ”) to match small sensor voltages to ADC input range ŌĆö improves effective resolution.
- **Sample-and-Hold (S/H):** Switch + capacitor. Holds analog value steady during ADC conversion.
- **ADC:** Successive-approximation or sigma-delta type. Produces N-bit digital code.
- **FIFO:** Hardware buffer to handle bus-latency mismatch.

**Single-Ended (SE) vs Differential (DI) Inputs (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Single-Ended (SE) | Differential (DI) |
|---|---|---|
| **Connections** | Signal + Common Ground (per channel 1 wire) | Signal+ and SignalŌłÆ (2 wires per channel) |
| **Channels** | 2├Ś density (e.g., 16 SE = 8 DI) | Half the density |
| **Noise Rejection** | Poor ŌĆö picks up ground-loop noise | Excellent ŌĆö common-mode noise rejected by CMRR |
| **Ground Loops** | Likely | Avoided |
| **Use Case** | Lab, clean signals < 1 V, > 1 m cable | Industrial, long cables, thermocouples, strain gauges |
| **Example** | NI USB-6009 has 8 SE / 4 DI | NI PCI-6221 has 16 DI or 32 SE |

**Selection Rule (KTU):** Use **DI** when (a) signal is small, (b) cable is long, (c) environment is electrically noisy, (d) ground potential difference > 1 V exists. Otherwise, **SE** is acceptable for higher channel density.

### ŌŁÉ QUESTION B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Signal Conditioning, DIO & Counters

#### **Q.B(a)** Explain the various signal conditioning operations required in a DAQ system. With a circuit diagram, describe the operation of an instrumentation amplifier. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Signal Conditioning Operations (4 marks):**

1. **Amplification** ŌĆö Boosts low-level signals (mV from thermocouple) to ADC range. Done by an **instrumentation amplifier** with high CMRR.
2. **Filtering** ŌĆö Removes noise. Anti-aliasing LPF with $f_c$ just above $f_{max}$ is mandatory.
3. **Isolation** ŌĆö Optocoupler or transformer provides galvanic isolation (typically 1500 V RMS). Protects PC and removes ground loops.
4. **Excitation** ŌĆö Stable voltage/current source for passive sensors:
   - Voltage excitation: 5 V / 10 V for strain-gauge bridges.
   - Current excitation: 4ŌĆō20 mA for RTDs (eliminates lead-resistance error).
5. **Linearization** ŌĆö Corrects non-linear sensor response using polynomial fit or lookup tables.
6. **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** ŌĆö Compensates the reference-junction voltage in thermocouples.
7. **Range Matching / Scaling** ŌĆö PGA adjusts gain to maximize ADC dynamic range.

**Instrumentation Amplifier (In-Amp) (3 marks):**

The classic three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier provides:
- High differential gain $A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$
- High **CMRR** (> 100 dB) at DC, decreasing with frequency.
- Very high input impedance on both $V_+$ and $V_-$ terminals.

**Working principle:** Input buffers (op-amps 1 and 2) feed a difference amplifier (op-amp 3). The gain-setting resistor $R_{gain}$ controls the differential gain. Common-mode voltage appears equally on both inputs and is rejected by the difference stage.

$$
V_{out} = G \cdot (V_+ - V_-), \quad G = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2}
$$

**Example:** For $R_1 = 25$ k╬®, $R_{gain} = 1$ k╬®, $R_2 = R_3 = 10$ k╬®:

$$
G = \left(1 + \frac{50{,}000}{1000}\right) \cdot 1 = 51
$$

---

#### **Q.B(b)** Explain the working of Digital I/O (DIO) and Counter/Timer subsystems in a DAQ device. How are counters used for frequency measurement? **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Digital I/O (DIO) Subsystem (3 marks):**

- Provides a group of **bidirectional or uni-directional TTL-compatible lines** (typically 8 or 32).
- Each line can be configured as input (read status of switch, button, sensor logic) or output (drive LED, relay, motor).
- **Handshake modes:** *Immediate* (no synchronization), *Strobed* (one line strobes data), *Burst* (continuous with clock).
- **Logic levels:** $V_{OH} \geq 2.4$ V, $V_{IH} \geq 2.0$ V, $V_{OL} \leq 0.4$ V, $V_{IL} \leq 0.8$ V (TTL).
- **Current drive:** ┬▒ 24 mA per line ŌĆö sufficient to drive small relays or optocouplers directly.

**Counter/Timer Subsystem (2 marks):**

- Built from cascaded digital counters fed by a high-frequency **timebase** (e.g., 20 MHz or 100 MHz oscillator).
- Used for:
  - **Event counting** (count pulses from a flow meter).
  - **Frequency measurement** (tachometer, RPM).
  - **Period measurement.**
  - **Pulse-width measurement.**
  - **Pulse generation** (PWM for motor control).

**Frequency Measurement using a Counter (2 marks):**

**Method ŌĆö Gate Time Measurement:**

1. Open the counter's **gate** for a precise duration $T_g$ (e.g., 1 second, generated by the timebase).
2. Count the number of input signal edges $N_{counts}$ during that gate.
3. Compute:

$$
f = \frac{N_{counts}}{T_g}
$$

4. **Resolution:** $\Delta f = 1/T_g$ (one count ambiguity).
5. **Accuracy:** Depends on timebase stability. Crystal timebase ŌåÆ 50 ppm typical.

**Example:** $N_{counts} = 10{,}000$ in $T_g = 1$ s ŌåÆ $f = 10$ kHz, $\Delta f = 1$ Hz, relative accuracy = 0.01%.

**Improvement:** Increase $T_g$ ŌåÆ smaller $\Delta f$ ŌåÆ finer resolution (at the cost of slower update rate).

## ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks in Module 4:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "Sampling Rate" with "ADC Clock Rate."** The ADC's *conversion* rate is a hardware limit; the *per-channel sampling rate* is reduced by multiplexing. Always state both.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the anti-aliasing filter.** A common 14-mark essay blunder: students describe the ADC and MUX but completely omit the **analog LPF before the S/H**. Expect 1ŌĆō2 marks lost.
> 
> 3. **Wrong formula for instrumentation amplifier gain.** Use the **three-resistor** form, not the non-inverting amplifier form.
> 
> 4. **Mixing up "single-ended" and "differential" definitions.** SE = signal w.r.t. ground. DI = signal difference between two wires.
> 
> 5. **Skipping units.** Every numeric answer in KTU carries a unit penalty if missing. Always write **Hz, V, mV/┬░C, dB, kS/s**.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting to "Stop + Clear" DAQ tasks** in LabVIEW block diagrams. This is a recurring **practical exam** deduction.
> 
> 7. **Miscounting DIO handshake modes.** KTU Module-4 specifically names *immediate, strobed, burst* ŌĆö memorise the three.
> 
> 8. **Writing "DAQ Assistant is a driver"** in theory questions. It is a *code generator / Express VI* sitting **on top of** the NI-DAQmx driver.

## ­¤öü Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU Module-4 revision checklist:**

- ­¤ö╣ A **transducer** converts physical ŌåÆ electrical energy. Active = self-generating; Passive = needs excitation.
- ­¤ö╣ **Signals** are analog (continuous) or digital (discrete). The DAQ's job is analog ŌåÆ digital conversion.
- ­¤ö╣ **Signal conditioning** = amplification + filtering + isolation + linearization + excitation + CJC.
- ­¤ö╣ **Instrumentation amplifier** has 3 op-amps, high CMRR, gain = $(1 + 2R_1/R_g)(R_3/R_2)$.
- ­¤ö╣ **Nyquist theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ ŌĆö use an anti-aliasing LPF before the ADC.
- ­¤ö╣ **ADC resolution:** $\Delta V = V_{FS}/2^N$. **Quantization error:** $\pm \Delta V/2$. **SQNR:** $6.02N + 1.76$ dB.
- ­¤ö╣ **AI modes:** Single-Ended (high density, noisy) vs Differential (low noise, half density).
- ­¤ö╣ **AO:** DAC + reconstruction LPF. Used for control signals, waveform generation.
- ­¤ö╣ **DIO:** TTL-compatible, ┬▒24 mA, modes ŌĆö immediate, strobed, burst.
- ­¤ö╣ **Counters:** frequency = $N_{counts}/T_g$; resolution = $1/T_g$. Use longer gates for better accuracy.
- ­¤ö╣ **DAQ software architecture:** Sensor ŌåÆ Driver (NI-DAQmx) ŌåÆ DAQ Assistant (Express VI) ŌåÆ LabVIEW VI ŌåÆ Display.
- ­¤ö╣ **DAQmx task lifecycle:** Create ŌåÆ Add Channel ŌåÆ Configure Timing ŌåÆ Configure Trigger ŌåÆ Start ŌåÆ Read/Write ŌåÆ Stop ŌåÆ Clear.
- ­¤ö╣ **Channel vs Task:** A *virtual channel* is a single named input/output. A *task* is a collection of one or more channels with shared timing/triggering.
- ­¤ö╣ **Components of CBMS:** Sensor + Signal Conditioning + DAQ Hardware + Driver + Application Software + Display.
- ­¤ö╣ Always check the **aggregate throughput** of multiplexed AI: $f_{agg} = n \times f_s \leq f_{ADC,max}$.
- ­¤ö╣ Common KTU devices: NI USB-6008/6009 (low-cost), NI PCI-6221/6251 (M-series), NI PXIe-6368 (high-end).
- ­¤ö╣ **LabVIEW practicality:** Always wire **error clusters**; always call **DAQmx Stop + Clear** at task end.
